data.title: Ed Miliband overrules locals to approve Britain’s biggest solar farm
data.description:
Ed Miliband has approved Britain’s biggest solar farm despite objections from nearby residents who have likened it to Chernobyl.

The Springwell Solar Farm in Lincolnshire will have a capacity of 800 megawatts, enough to power some 180,000 homes a year, the Government said on Wednesday.

The development, proposed by EDF and Luminous Energy, is the 25th large solar farm approved by Labour since 2024 using planning powers that allow ministers to overrule communities when a project is deemed “nationally significant”.

It has proved divisive in Lincolnshire, where residents turned out in large numbers to object to the plans at various public hearings.

It will cover seven square miles of farmland in solar panels – an area 10 times greater than London’s Hyde Park – and affect 10 local villages and thousands of residents.

Residents claimed the loss of prime farmland and the effect on communities was too high a price to pay for the green energy the project would provide.

Local politicians had also accused Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, of having “made up his mind already” despite fears the scheme “would change the very nature of Lincolnshire”.

In 2024, Labour scrapped planning rules that blocked the construction of new solar farms on food-producing land.

Mr Miliband also designated large solar and wind farms as “nationally significant” schemes that planners should approve by default.

Marc Williams, of the Springwell Solar Farm Action Group, told Lincolnshire Live last May: “The community are so concerned about this ... Everyone’s against it, apart from those who will profit.

“We wouldn’t object to plans for a couple of hundred acres but this is vast. It will be an industrialised complex like Chernobyl.

“People will go for a drive and see nothing but panels.”

‘Bulldoze the blockers’

Ministers said the scheme would boost supplies of “home-grown” electricity at a time when the Middle East crisis had “exposed the cost of relying on fossil fuel markets Britain does not control”.

Michael Shanks, the energy minister, said: “We are driving further and faster for clean home-grown power that we control to protect the British people and bring down bills for good.

“It is crucial we learn the lessons of the conflict in the Middle East – solar is one of the cheapest forms of power available and is how we get off the roller-coaster of international fossil fuel markets and secure our own energy independence.”

Mr Miliband has been leading government efforts to make Britain’s electricity system run on 95pc clean power by 2030, vowing to “double down” on the Government’s net zero policies since the outbreak of the Iran war.

However, many of the solar and onshore wind farms proposed in rural areas of England are facing stiff resistance from local residents. Labour has repeatedly insisted it will push through green energy infrastructure despite this opposition.

data.title: Best cash Isas: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
The start of a new tax year is a great time for Isas. As savers get their new Isa allowance, we’re seeing cash Isa providers compete for your cash with increasingly generous rates.

A cash Isa is similar to a savings account, but with a crucial difference – you don’t pay tax on the interest you earn.

It is one of four types of adult individual savings accounts (Isas) – the others are stocks and shares Isas, innovative finance Isas and lifetime Isas.

You can currently save up to £20,000 per tax year, which is known as the Isa allowance. In the 2025 Budget, Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced plans to reduce this to £12,000 from April 2027 for savers under the age of 65; younger savers will have to use an investment Isa if they wish to use the remaining £8,000 of their allowance.

You can either deposit this sum into one account or spread it across several, and you can transfer your Isa savings between providers if you want to take advantage of a better rate elsewhere.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains how cash Isas work, covering:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best cash Isas in April
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa
  • Why choose a cash Isa?
  • Cash Isa FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables below show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best cash Isas in April 2026

Find the best cash Isa for you using the tables below.

Best easy-access cash Isas

These accounts usually allow you to make as many deposits and withdrawals as you like – but this flexibility can come with lower rates. For the savings equivalent, you can see our guide to the best easy-access savings accounts.

Best variable rate cash Isas

Accounts in the table below show the best Isa rates on the market for accounts that offer a variable rate of interest. While this could increase at any time – which can be valuable when rates are on an upward trend – it could also decrease.

Best notice cash Isas

These accounts require you to give a certain amount of notice to your provider when you want to make a withdrawal, indicating the number of days you must wait until your money is released. You can usually make as many withdrawals as you like – but be sure to check the account terms first.

If this way of saving suits you, make sure you check our guide to the best notice accounts for alternative options.

Best fixed-rate cash Isas

These Isas require you to lock your money away for a specified period of time. The rate of interest is guaranteed throughout this period, which can give you peace of mind should other rates take a tumble.

For the savings account equivalent, which you might want to use if you’ve used up your Isa allowance, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For other types of tax-free saving, see our guide to the best Junior cash Isas for under-18s. Or, if you’re saving to buy a home or fund your retirement, don’t miss our guide to the best lifetime Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa

Choosing the best cash Isa for you can be tricky, as it depends on why you’re saving and how you want to save – but there should be options to suit everyone.

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare, said: “For savers that are more tax savvy, cash Isas may be the ideal option as you receive a £20,000 Isa allowance each year and can pay into multiple Isas within the same period providing this limit is not exceeded. Isas aren’t too dissimilar to their traditional savings counterparts with easy access, notice, fixed and regular accounts being an option.

“It is worth considering that for the variable rate options, interest could increase or decrease over time, and those Isas that are fixed for an agreed term will either not allow earlier access or may deduct a certain amount of interest.”

Cash Isas can also be an effective way of saving for the longer term.

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Cash Isas have certainly seen a resurgence of late. Looking at what may be coming in Labour’s Budget, and the rhetoric around higher taxes, cash Isas should be the first port of call for all savers at the moment.

“If you’ve got £20,000 or less, definitely go for a cash Isa first and ensure your money is protected from tax. If you’re fortunate enough to have more than £20,000 to save in an Isa, then you can look at other saving accounts as well.”

Why choose a cash Isa?

Cash Isas are beneficial for many savers as they offer an easy way to make sure your savings interest is sheltered from income tax.

Their popularity took a dip when the personal savings allowance (PSA) was introduced in 2016, as it means some savers can earn up to £1,000 in tax-free savings interest each tax year. When interest rates are low, only those with large savings pots or high incomes come into the scope of paying tax on savings interest.

However, savings rates have been high for some time; until recently it was not uncommon to be earning 5pc. Someone with a £1,000 PSA could face a savings tax bill with £20,000 in savings – a problem that doesn’t occur with a cash Isa. What’s more, your growth is protected as it compounds for years into the future, which has helped some savers become Isa millionaires.

The main issue that comes with a cash Isa is the £20,000 Isa limit on how much you can pay into it each tax year. If you want to move a large savings deposit in order to protect your returns from tax, it may need to be moved gradually over several tax years.

In some cases, interest rates offered by cash Isas can be lower than their savings equivalent. Providers are well aware of the extra tax benefit Isas offer, and know they don’t have to be as competitive with rates.

It’s important to shop around and find the most competitive rate you can. Cash Isa rates can be lower than their savings account counterparts, but the tax-free benefits of an Isa will often outweigh the loss of a little interest.

If you’re not sure whether you could be in danger of having to pay tax on your savings interest, try the Telegraph Money savings tax calculator – simply enter your savings, interest rate and tax band and it can tell you whether you’d be better off getting an Isa.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no limit to the number of cash Isas you can have – but it’s a good idea not to have so many that you lose track of them.

You can hold cash Isas you’ve paid into in past tax years, and – now that Isa rules have changed – you can also open and pay into as many as you like in the same tax year. The key thing is to make sure you don’t pay in more than £20,000 across all Isas in the same tax year.

Yes, you can combine Isas into one account – but in order to do this without affecting the tax status of the money, or your Isa allowance, you must move the cash via an Isa transfer.

There are quite a few rules to bear in mind when you do this – you can find out more in our comprehensive guide to Isa transfers.

This depends on the rate you’re earning and the rate of inflation. At the time of writing, lots of cash Isas can beat inflation, but you might need to shop around for them. It’s a good idea to keep an eye on what’s happening with inflation versus the best Isa rates so you can gauge whether or not you have a competitive deal.

Our inflation calculator shows the effect rising prices can have on your savings.

It’s never too late to put money in an Isa to benefit from its tax-free status. However, if you’re planning to deposit money very close to the end of the tax year on April 5, there’s a chance you could miss the deadline.

If this happens, you can still save the money – it will just be taken from the following year’s allowance instead.

The annual Isa allowance resets at the start of a tax year on April 6. You can choose to put this entire amount into one account, or split it among several.

Mr Hicks said: “Isas were typically seasonal, with a big spike in demand around the end of the tax year. They are much less timely now and every year the cash Isa season gets longer and longer.”

data.title: ‘Not worried’ about shadow banking risk, says JP Morgan boss
data.description:
The boss of JP Morgan has said that he is “not worried” about his own bank’s $50bn (£37bn) exposure to private credit, despite recent warnings over the risks of “shadow banking”.

Jamie Dimon said that JP Morgan was well insulated from any wider ructions in the private credit sector.

The chief executive told analysts: “You’re going to have very large losses in private credit before it looks like banks are going to get hit. 

“It doesn’t mean you won’t feel some stress and strain, and you might have to do something about it, but I’m not particularly worried about it.”

His comments come despite growing alarm over the private credit industry, following a series of high-profile failures of shadow bank-backed businesses.

Private credit is a form of shadow banking, where loans come from non-banking sources such as hedge funds, which are subject to less strict regulations. The sector has grown rapidly since the financial crisis, as tighter regulations on traditional banks pushed riskier forms of finance into unregulated markets.

However, concerns have been mounting over loose lending standards and the opacity of the sector, with Lloyd Blankfein, the former boss of Goldman Sachs, saying earlier this year that he could “smell” a crash on the horizon.

Andrew Bailey called for a review on Tuesday of how private credit and other non-banks are regulated.

The governor of the Bank of England said the current regime needed to “have its tyres kicked”, adding there needed to be clear distinctions between protecting trust in money while avoiding the need to bail out institutions that make risky bets.

Speaking in New York, he said: “It remains an open question to consider where are the limits of what an independent central bank should do in the financial stability space.”

In the past, Mr Dimon has warned about the private credit sector, saying more “cockroaches” could emerge after the collapse of the sub-prime auto lender Tricolor and the car parts supplier First Brands.

Both companies were heavy users of private credit. JP Morgan took a $170m hit from the collapse of Tricolor.

The US banking giant said in its first-quarter results that its overall exposure to the private credit sector was about $50bn.

Mr Dimon had also warned in his annual letter at the start of this month that losses from private credit were likely to be larger than expected, as lending standards in the sector had been “modestly weakening pretty much across the board”.

In a call with analysts on Tuesday, Mr Dimon reiterated the point. However, he said that any losses were unlikely to be systemic given that, at $1.7tn, the private credit market was still small compared with other forms of lending.

“It almost can’t be systemic at that size relative to anything else,” he said.

Mr Dimon was speaking as JP Morgan reported a 13pc increase in net profits to $16.5bn in the first three months of the year. Volatility in markets, fuelled by the war in Iran, led the bank to report its highest-ever trading revenue of $11.6bn, surpassing the bank’s previous record by almost $2bn and marking a 20pc increase on last year.

It follows similar increases in profits at rival Wall Street banks, which are also benefiting from their role financing trades during the Middle East turmoil.

Between them, JP Morgan, Citi and Wells Fargo made more than $25bn in profits during the first three months of the year.

data.title: How to make your investments Trump-proof
data.description:
There was a grim inevitability to the everything, everywhere market rally of the last few years turning into the (nearly) everything, everywhere market sell-off.

Most stock markets have shot up in value in the past few years, including, whisper it quietly, our own.

Then along came the Iran war, and many of those gains vanished. That leaves investors with a more tactical mindset in a quandary: even after the ceasefire, how to hedge against the certainty of future uncertainty?

None of us knows what will happen with Iran, including whether the ceasefire will hold. But we do know, with some degree of uncertainty, that inflation is increasing, food crises are looming and energy costs have risen sharply. How to manage this risk?

A good place to start is with the evidence so far. This defies conventional investment logic, proving that the past is no guide to the future. Long-trusted hedges against geopolitical risk and market uncertainty have crumbled on contact.

No major asset class offered any meaningful protection before the ceasefire: not bonds, not gold, not tech stocks or even domestically oriented UK stocks via the FTSE 250 index.

Nearly everything has fallen, and none of the traditional hedges has worked – with one obvious exception, namely, global energy stocks, with the MSCI World energy index up 6.8pc. Even this last sector has stumbled in the last few days as markets begin to think through a possible deal.

That said, even if there is an eventual deal over Iran, most commentators expect the volatility in energy markets to remain high, and prices to remain above levels seen before the conflict. That suggests energy stocks might still be a useful hedge.

The flip side of this strength is that those bumper profits will almost certainly attract political attention.

The strategist Louis-Vincent Gave, of Gavekal, the widely followed research firm, warned last week of blowback against the energy industry.

He wrote to clients that in “Europe, this might mean one-off taxes on exceptional profits. In the US ... it might mean price controls and export restrictions”.

As Gave noted, “incidentally, and perhaps unsurprisingly, China already seems to be there”, with restrictions on exports of fuel and fertilisers.

For me, the interesting failure so far has been hedge funds: all the major hedge fund strategies, as covered by the HFRU composite indices, fell away in March, with the biggest losses for global macro strategies, ie investors trying to second-guess big structural shifts.

This, I think, underscores the uniqueness of the current Trump-led world. Who would try to second-guess what the US president says in his next Truth Social message?

Trend-following strategies, commonly known as managed futures or CTA strategies, are also struggling as market narratives shift so quickly. One month, we were all set for lower interest rates; next month, those rates are suddenly heading higher.

That said, there is some academic evidence suggesting that if we are heading into a world of higher inflation and possibly lower growth, hedge funds might be better able to navigate this uncertainty, though I’d attach a big warning to that statement: “depending on what Trump does next”.

That potentially more stagflationary world order – higher, more persistent inflation, and lower GDP growth as consumers swallow increased prices – brings us to two classic hedges against risk: gold and bonds. The sell-off in gold isn’t a huge surprise to most observers. Gold and silver had become hype-driven and had overshot, and the correction is being driven by huge retail investors selling of ETFs.

That underscores an observation from the recent, seminal UBS Global Investment Returns Yearbook by UK academics. This found that “of the 28 years in which inflation exceeded 3pc, we find that gold returns were negative in 13 of them”. Nevertheless, the academics balanced this by saying that their data show “that gold has been more effective at beating inflation over the long term”.

Put simply, don’t lazily assume that if inflation rises over the next 12 months, gold prices will shoot up again, although gold remains a useful diversifier if inflation rates stay consistently high for a number of years. Also, gold bulls argue that it might benefit from investor concerns about ever-expanding fiscal deficits among the major G7 governments.

Which brings us to the classic diversifier of risk: bonds. Bonds haven’t provided quite the protection that you might have expected, bar inflation-linked securities, which have held their own in price terms.

If the outcome of the present crisis is higher inflation, then most conventional bonds could be in trouble. In fact, I find it surprising how few strategists I talk to think bonds have much value beyond inflation-linked bonds, which could be a contrarian signal if you are a cynic about pointy-head types.

However, returning to that UBS study, I would point out that the academics crunched data for 125 years during periods of high and low inflation and found that bonds provided negative real returns in all scenarios where inflation was high, while equities outperformed, and bonds only provided positive real returns where inflation was “middling or lower”, as the academics put it.

As an aside, the real killer scenario for bonds is one where inflation is high and economic growth rates much lower – a scenario that grows more likely by the day.

I’d finish with two suggestions, one based on the stagflation scenario, the other on increased market turbulence. If you think stagflation is very likely, then a recent analysis by Schroders’ Duncan Lamont is a useful guide.

He’s dug around in similar data sets to those of UBS researchers and found that, in a stagflationary world, equities generally perform worse during stagflation than at other times, but the difference is not statistically meaningful, and they have historically kept pace with inflation and beaten cash.

In terms of sectors, investors might reasonably expect energy/mining, utilities, and healthcare stocks to do well, while avoiding tech and financials.

I’m not completely convinced by the stagflation arguments. We could, for instance, lunge into a recession, or AI could produce a burst of growth. Not every scenario points to stagflation.

But plenty of scenarios in the next few years do point to my main worry: market turbulence driven by Mr Trump.

That will prompt many peaks and troughs, which will be fantastic news for my last suggested hedge: the broking businesses that make money from turbulent markets.

AJ Bell is up 12.8pc in March; CMC Markets up 6.83pc; and Plus500 up just under 2pc. This means they have proved fantastic hedges in the current market turmoil.

These businesses make more money not only as interest rates rise but as investors trade more often. They are my preferred, highly profitable, cash-generating hedges.

data.title: Ovo Energy sues TalkTalk over botched takeover
data.description:
Ovo Energy has sued troubled broadband giant TalkTalk over a botched deal to buy up customers.

The energy company has launched a legal claim in a dispute about the sale of broadband customers to TalkTalk, as Ovo scrambles to secure a sale.

Ovo sold SSE Energy Services’s phone and broadband business in 2022, which saw 135,000 customers transferred to TalkTalk.

The company had acquired the telecoms business following its takeover of SSE Energy Services, a gas and electricity provider, two years previously.

To complete the deal, TalkTalk paid an initial sum upfront and agreed to make further contributions once certain milestones were met.

However, the deal soured after swathes of customers left TalkTalk, rendering the takeover largely pointless. TalkTalk has since refused to pay the outstanding sum to Ovo, prompting the company to launch a legal claim.

The lawsuit underscores efforts by Ovo to settle its affairs as bankers scramble to complete a sale.

Ovo, which is Britain’s fourth-largest gas and electricity supplier with four million customers, has been attempting to raise £300m in fresh investment for months in an effort to shore up its finances.

The company is facing uncertainty over its future after failing to meet financial stress tests introduced by regulator Ofgem in the wake of the 2022 energy crisis, which caused dozens of suppliers to collapse.

Bankers at Rothschild have been appointed to oversee the fundraising process, with a sale of the business also under consideration. It is thought that any deal will not include Ovo’s technology arm Kaluza.

Ovo has also begun cutting tens of millions of pounds in costs under a new business plan aimed at securing its survival.

Despite warnings about its ability to stay afloat and posting losses of £135m in 2024, Ovo still paid out £27m that year to a company owned by Stephen Fitzpatrick, its founder.

Mr Fitzpatrick, who founded Ovo in 2009, has extracted tens of millions of pounds through a licensing deal to use the brand. The energy company last year bought the brand outright in a £150m deal.

Mr Fitzpatrick’s other ventures include Kensington Roof Gardens, a private members’ club in west London once owned by Sir Richard Branson.

The legal claim will create a problem for TalkTalk, which is struggling under its £1.4bn debt burden.

Surging interest costs have pushed the company to heavy losses and caused it to fall behind on payments to suppliers including Openreach, BT’s network division.

The troubles have prompted shareholders, including Ares Management and Sir Charles Dunstone, the executive chairman of the broadband giant, to provide repeated cash injections to keep the company afloat. The latest £115m lifeline in March marked the third such deal since the end of 2024.

TalkTalk is also seeking a buyer for part or whole of the business. Larger rival Virgin Media O2 had been considering a deal for its consumer division but has now walked away.

Ovo and TalkTalk declined to comment.

data.title: The National Trust gift shop has lost its way
data.description:
Spring may be when a young man’s fancy lightly turns to love, but people of other age groups – parents of small children, the retired, the middle-aged who have elderly relatives to care for – are just as likely to be turning their focus towards the National Trust, whose properties are perfect for their needs. A well-tended garden, a bit of culture, a good tea and a visit to the shop: the combination is, or ought to be, a recipe for happiness.

Alas, one or perhaps two elements of the formula are letting the side down. The shop is almost invariably a disappointment. To judge from comments on Trustpilot, quite a large constituency thinks the offering is generic, little different from the high street except for costing more. Does the acorn on the label guarantee distinctiveness, or support for local crafts and tradespeople? Usually, the answer seems to be no.

I’m not knocking fridge magnets. Cloth badges which can be sewn onto rucksacks have their place. A mouse-themed Nativity set may be in questionable taste but that’s not my beef. 

Founded in 1895, the National Trust emerged from the same Ruskin-inspired mindset that gave rise to the Arts and Crafts movement. In an increasingly homogenised 20th century, it held its ground as a last redoubt of local identity. Now, it appears to have gone over to the dark side, becoming a brand that is not significantly different from John Lewis. 

I’m all for John Lewis – it’s the staff of life. I’d just like the NT to be something different.

It could become a champion for British craft, for example. This would be in the spirit of houses such as Snowshill, whose eccentric owner in the early 20th century was the architect and collector Charles Paget Wade – an Arts and Crafts figure if ever there was one. As William Morris, the main founder of the Arts and Crafts movement, knew all too well, craftsmanship can be expensive; although a socialist, he found himself compelled to cater to the “swinish luxury of the rich”.

But it isn’t as though the prices charged by the NT are cheap. A basket or rug from a local maker might be within the price bracket of other products currently being offered. Admittedly, a throw made from recycled plastic bottles only comes in at £20, but that raises another question. What about natural materials?

Or the best in design? A generation ago, the NT would be teased about its ubiquitous tea towels, but they were much loved and recognised as being first in class – the work, very often, of Pat Albeck. Now the organisation is famous instead for ruthless branding, which uses its power in the market to promote its own lines to the exclusion of most else.

Buying is presumably done centrally because it’s cheaper that way, but the range in each shop looks the same. There are exceptions like Sissinghurst and Waddesdon where the strong involvement of a family adds personality. Plants often come from the gardens where they’re sold. But mostly what appears to count is the bottom line.

This may seem fair enough because charities must be businesslike. But isn’t it possible that the NT would make even more money, through legacies and donations, if it became synonymous with the old-fashioned excellence that can’t be found elsewhere, rather than brand-centric values that bore us all to death?

data.title: If Labour is serious about growth, it must open up the North Sea
data.description:
One consequence of the current surge in global energy prices is that it has turned the spotlight onto Britain’s policy with regard to its unexploited supplies of fossil fuels. Sixty-five leading climate scientists recently wrote an open letter to the Financial Times arguing against new drilling in the North Sea. They said that this would imperil the fight against climate change while bringing no economic benefit. Do they have a point?

The argument primarily concerns the Jackdaw and Rosebank fields. These two fields are not covered by the ban on new licenses since their applications had already been submitted when Labour took office.

The issue here is not climate science which, despite the claim that everything in this subject is settled, remains controversial. But we don’t need to get into that. Rather, the issue here is about economics.

The climate authors advance several arguments. One is that 90pc of the energy reserves in the North Sea have already been exploited. This is true but so what? Why does this mean that we should leave the remaining 10pc unexploited?

Another is that it is not worth opening up new supplies from the North Sea because this would have little or no effect on the prices of gas and oil here because these are set globally. This is true but allowing extra domestic production would create more jobs in the parts of the country that have suffered from the rundown of the North Sea industry and produce increased tax revenues.

The UK’s trade deficit would be correspondingly reduced. So long as this activity is profitable, it will bring benefit to the UK economy. And the people to judge whether or not it is profitable are the energy companies, which want to have the chance to exploit these two North Sea fields.

Indeed, it would be wrong to use the availability of domestic supplies to keep prices down artificially. Domestic consumers of energy should face world prices for energy. In that way they will be incentivised to minimise their consumption of it. But profitable domestic energy supply can improve UK economic performance such that the Government could give relief to consumers in other ways, rather than artificially keeping the price down.

The great irony about our restrictions on UK fossil fuel production is that it is not unambiguously reducing emissions. We import 35pc of our gas from Norway. The fact that this gas is produced there rather than in the UK does nothing to reduce carbon emissions. Indeed, this imported gas may generate higher emissions than our own domestically produced gas. This is the height of absurdity.

Some defenders of the current policy claim that renewable energy is cheaper. If that were true, than we surely ought to rush headlong towards replacing all fossil fuel energy with renewables. But it isn’t. Admittedly, it is true that the marginal cost of renewable energy is often lower than for fossil fuel produced energy. But this ignores the fact that because of the intermittency of renewables – sometimes the wind does not blow and/or the sun does not shine – there has to be huge and expensive duplicate capacity.

An argument that we hear often is that the UK’s energy policy is setting an example to the world. This betrays an exaggerated view of the UK’s importance in both global energy markets and in the world more generally. Indeed, it harks back to the days of empire. We are now only a medium-sized economy.

Admittedly, there is scheduled to be a major international conference on climate change later this month in Columbia. The UK will be one of about 50 countries represented. Allowing new drilling in the North Sea could potentially cause the UK government considerable embarrassment at this forum.

But so what? What is the market price of embarrassment? The big boys in global energy are the US and China. Needless to say, they are continuing to consume and produce large amounts of energy from fossil fuels. For all the effect our policy has on them, we might as well wave a union jack over the North Sea. (Come to think of it, though, this might cause distress and therefore
requires a trigger warning.)

Allowing drilling in the Jackdaw and Rosebank fields would not in itself transform UK energy supply or the economy more generally. But it would be a start. The Government repeatedly tells us that its overriding ambition is to deliver decent economic growth for the UK. And within this, it sees manufacturing as key. Yet the UK has some of the highest industrial energy prices in the developed world. They have recently been three times America’s. Scarce wonder, then, that UK manufacturing has been in the doldrums.

But the damage does not stop there. The Government is relying on the UK’s prominent position in AI to deliver decent economic growth. Yet AI is a huge consumer of energy. Worryingly, OpenAI has recently announced that it is halting its UK data centre project, citing high energy cost and concerns about regulation.

By wanting to secure cheap energy for the UK, this does not mean that we necessarily reject the whole policy agenda against global warming. The issue is about the pace of change, the cost, and how a medium-sized economy should behave when the big boys are setting the pace. Even climate change sceptics would surely acknowledge that it would be good if we can eventually wean ourselves off reliance on fossil fuels, because so much of the world’s supplies of gas and oil are located in places with hostile governments (Russia) or in areas of instability (the Middle East).

Yet this does not mean that we should impoverish ourselves in the process.

Roger Bootle is senior independent adviser to Capital Economics and a senior fellow at Policy Exchange. roger.bootle@capitaleconomics.com

data.title: Why Britain faces the growing risk of Spanish-style blackouts
data.description:
The great British blackout was once a threat that hung over the country during the depths of winter, when generators struggled to meet demand or storms destroyed power lines.

But today, as the net zero revolution rolls on, the greatest threat to grid stability could land on a sunny Sunday afternoon.

According to the National Energy System Operator (Neso), the body responsible for keeping the lights on, the UK power grid is at increasing risk of being overwhelmed by surges in solar power.

Underpinning this threat is the fact that too many solar panels have been connected to the grid without any way of monitoring them or controlling their output.

As a result, a typically sunny day in summer can lead to nearly 19 gigawatts (GW) of power pouring into the UK’s networks, while only 12-15GW is being consumed.

That imbalance is a recipe for disaster because significant destabilisation of the grid can cause blackouts.

Such a calamity struck Spain and Portugal last year, when a chain reaction triggered by solar farms knocked out power to 55 million people.

At the time, solar and wind generated 80pc of energy in the Iberian Peninsula, the result of renewables displacing power stations run by nuclear and gas.

While a welcome switch for supporters of green energy, the removal of traditional power stations can prove problematic – as Spain and Portugal discovered last year.

This is because of their importance in providing vital system stability, known as inertia.

Inertia is created by generators with spinning parts – such as turbines running on gas, coal or hydropower – which wind and solar do not have.

This form of kinetic energy helps to prevent sudden changes in a system’s frequency.

Therefore, when two solar farms suddenly disconnected from Spain’s grid last year, there was no steady inertia to keep the country’s power networks afloat.

In practice, Neso is confident the UK will never see such a disaster. However, it admits the challenge is growing as ever more solar farms are built in Ed Miliband’s net zero drive.

“We are confident that we have the tools required to operate the system safely and reliably,” Neso said in its Summer Outlook report.

“But there may be periods when balancing supply, demand and system needs will require us to use our full range of operational tools, including system notices.”

However, independent experts are not so sure – and we may soon find out who is right. The crunch will probably come around the late May bank holiday, or on any sunny summer Sunday.

Last year, that date saw national demand for power plummet to a record low of 11GW, while sun and wind drove up power generation to nearly 19GW. And crucially, Neso could do little to stop it.

This sense of powerlessness is new. In the past, the grid operator’s response to an excess of power was simply to switch off some power stations.

But Neso’s problem is that it can no longer see what is happening on the UK’s power networks.

Unlike before, energy is being created naturally from wind and solar farms that Neso cannot control.

Underlying Britain’s energy grid are two main power networks.

At the top is the high-voltage transmission grid, which takes electricity from power stations to cities, towns and industry.

Everything on it is closely measured and monitored from Neso’s Electricity National Control Centre in Wokingham, Berkshire.

There, controllers use a giant wall-to-wall digital map of the transmission network to match supply to demand – switching power stations on or reducing output as needed.

Below that lies the distribution network, which takes electricity from the transmission grid, reduces its voltage, and pumps it into our homes and businesses.

It was designed to be a passive power carrier, meaning it lacks most of the switching or measuring systems found on the transmission grid. Ultimately, this makes it hard to monitor or control.

However, in the rush for decarbonisation, officials have been hooking the distribution system up to hundreds of new power sources, particularly solar farms.

Almost all of those solar generators are largely invisible to Neso’s operators, and they cannot see how much power is being generated or switch it off.

This now poses a problem, according to Simon Gallagher, the chief executive of UK Network Services, a leading consultancy.

“Traditionally, most power generators were connected to the transmission system and ‘dispatched’ centrally, so Neso can instruct them to turn on, turn off, ramp up and down to balance the grid in real time,” he says.

“Big power stations with large gas generators get tuned up and down as Neso monitors changes in demand.

“But solar is almost all connected to the distribution system and Neso has no control or even visibility of what it is doing. It means there is a risk of imbalances. On a sunny summer Sunday, demand will be very low, but solar will be generating at full capacity.”

However, whether this results in a blackout similar to Spain is another matter.

Some industry experts say an energy crisis is unlikely in the UK because its grid is structured differently, but regional failures and high costs remain a threat.

“Britain’s solar surplus problem is more about balancing costs than sudden system failure,” says Timothy Santonastaso, the deputy head of European power pricing at the consultancy Argus.

Such problems should have been addressed sooner, according to Claire Coutinho, the energy secretary in the last Conservative government and now a shadow minister.

‘Bogus numbers’

While in power, Coutinho says she became so worried about the impact of excessive solar disrupting the grid that she ordered civil servants to investigate.

This involved a review of the costs associated with balancing the grid when it is too windy or sunny.

Last year, the Government spent more than £1bn on paying wind farms to switch off in so-called curtailment costs.

“I started a full systems cost of wind and solar in the department because we need to know the true costs of renewables to consumers,” she says.

“I asked officials to prove to me that renewables were cheap and then realised their numbers were bogus.”

Coutinho claims Miliband has since cancelled her review. She also says the Energy Secretary is paying too much for net zero.

A spokesman for Miliband rejected the criticisms, instead claiming that solar and wind farms would bring huge benefits. “We are reversing decades of underinvestment and delivering the biggest upgrade to the grid in decades, which will minimise constraint costs, meet the capacity needed to deliver clean power by 2030 and help bring down bills for good,” he said.

He also played down the threat of grid disruption. “The National Energy System Operator has always had to manage changes in demand between the winter and summer months. They have well-established tools to manage the grid, including during times of low demand, which will not impact supply.”

The fresh row over solar is just the latest front in the battle over green energy.

Within Labour, Miliband’s renewables rollout has delighted the party faithful and made him increasingly popular among members.

However, it has also turned energy and climate policy, an area on which there was general political agreement until two years ago, into a culture war.

Leading the charge against Labour is Reform UK, which has pledged to disconnect solar farms if it wins power at the next general election.

“These solar farms are being built for the benefit of foreign investors, but they are destroying our countryside and ruining our environment,” says Richard Tice, Reform’s energy spokesman.

“It’s my living mission to stop this madness. Britain has done its bit – we’ve cut emissions by 50pc and impoverished ourselves doing it. Now’s the time to make Britain prosperous again, and that means cheap energy, lots of it, whether from gas, nuclear or oil.”

No doubt Miliband will disagree. However, the latest warning from Neso may at least make him sit up and notice.

data.title: Heathrow’s new car parks to cost more than Gatwick’s second runway
data.description:
From HS2 to Crossrail, British infrastructure projects lead the world in one unenviable respect: their sizeable, often runaway budgets.

The bill for big-ticket construction schemes typically starts off with eye-watering sums – and then climb to even more staggering levels as budgets are blown and costs run rampant.

Now a plan to provide 36,000 parking spaces at Heathrow airport is poised to join the pantheon of cash-hungry mega-projects after it was revealed that it will come with a staggering £2.6bn price tag.

That’s £400m more than the estimated outlay for a new runway at Gatwick and more than half the cost of Heathrow’s Terminal 5 – the largest free-standing structure in the UK.

At £72,000 per parking space, the two multi-storey car parks planned under the scheme will also be comfortably the most expensive in Britain and a contender for the costliest ever built.

Heathrow argues the price per space will be lower because only half the overall cost, about £1.3bn, is for the buildings themselves, with the rest of the spending going towards associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Yet the airport has faced criticism from some of the world’s largest airlines, who will ultimately pick up the bill for the world’s most expensive car park.

In a week when National Car Parks (NCP), the country’s biggest car park operator, collapsed into administration, the plan might seem an ambitious one.

However, unlike NCP, which was hit by a drop in commuting, Heathrow can rely on a captive customer base and funding rules that mean construction costs are ultimately likely to be passed on as aircraft landing fees.

Airline bosses have attacked the project as typical of Heathrow’s largesse in lavishing cash on gold-plated enhancements that they and their customers must ultimately pay for.

The airport says the car parks will be a vital component of its £49bn plan for a third runway that will lift passenger numbers from 84 million a year to as many as 150 million.

It says the sites – one next to the M4 and the other to the south near the M25 – will be needed to cope with the thousands of additional passengers driving to what is already Europe’s busiest airport each day, even if a higher proportion of people take public transport.

And it argues that the expense of the project reflects construction costs in London that are themselves amongst the highest in the world.

But Luis Gallego, the boss of British Airways (BA) owner International Airlines Group, said the plan was overpriced.

He highlighted the shortcomings of a funding model that allows Heathrow to charge airlines a regulated fee to cover building and operating costs.

“We support expansion, but not at any cost. All future spending must be carefully controlled as Heathrow already charges passengers twice as much as Amsterdam and three times as much as Paris,” he said.

Willie Walsh, the director general of the International Air Transport Association, was blunter, describing the car parks plan as “madness” and saying his mind “boggled” at the £2.6bn bill.

He said Heathrow had proposed what would have been the world’s most expensive car park when he was chief executive of BA as part of a previous runway plan that was rejected in 2010.

He said: “You can find data on what it costs to build a car park, there’s lots of evidence there, and Heathrow is just way out of line with the rest of the world.

“The problem is that they are incentivised to spend as much money as possible and to build the most expensive car park and to be proud of that.”

Heathrow has said that calling the new structures car parks does not do them justice.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, has instead referred to them as “parking villages”.

The blueprint for the new runway proposes “two large parkway car parks” that would replace more than 20 existing facilities, with the northern site able to accommodate 20,000 vehicles and the southern one 16,000.

It adds: “This could support fast and efficient connections to each terminal campus via dedicated transport systems.

“These new parkways could also provide local communities with improved access to airport terminals and public transport links and help reduce congestion on local roads.”

While a leaked Heathrow briefing paper states the cost of the parkways to be £2.6bn, a spokesman said the parking areas alone will consume only about half the budget, with the rest covering the cost of associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Major profit centre

The car parks may be necessary to cope with an influx of new passengers, but they would also be a major profit centre, with parking fees contributing £200m in revenue last year.

Heathrow’s long-stay car parks currently charge £46.80 for the first day and £37.40 for each additional 24-hour period.

Taking an average of £40 per day, the number of spaces planned could generate more than £500m in revenue over the course of a year.

That is not including income from drop-off charges and the short-stay car parks nearer to terminals that the airport has indicated it plans to retain.

Michael O’Leary, the chief executive of Ryanair, which does not fly from Heathrow, said drop-off fees were “a scandal” across UK airports and that parking had been turned into a cash cow integral to their business models.

He said: “The more they earn from car parks the more they will dividend it out to their shareholders and then complain that they can’t make a reasonable return on the risks they were taking investing in runways in London.

“But there’s no risk at all, it’s a complete freebie, which is why pension funds now own most of these airports.”

O’Leary said the cost of runways, car parks and terminals was ultimately the result of a “broken” regulatory model that “rewards inefficiency and incentivises airports to squander gazillions”.

A study from Oxford Global Projects commissioned by Heathrow Reimagined, a lobby group formed by airlines including BA, has suggested that Heathrow’s construction costs across a variety of infrastructure including car parks are consistently among the highest in the world.

The report said the cost of the runway alone – covering cement, asphalt and lights – would come in at more than £800,000 per metre, making it one of the most expensive ever built.

Jonathan Simcock, author of The Delivery Gap, an examination of failed infrastructure projects, said companies like Heathrow have an incentive to inflate estimated costs by “including all the bells and whistles they can”.

In so doing, he said, they aim to secure regulatory approval for levels of spending – in Heathrow’s case to be funded by airlines and their customers – that will cover all eventualities and “ensure they are not left holding the baby”.

“If the estimate is inflated then the company ends up making an inappropriate profit. But if it’s undercooked the company gets into trouble, somebody has to bail them out and the country doesn’t get the infrastructure it needs,” he says.

Woldbye has said that the costings for the car parks and the wider runway project could change and that Heathrow is open to conversations about modifying its plans.

However, Simcock warned: “When the conversation is about how it can possibly cost that much, very often it turns out that it should have been, can you really do it for that?

“For very large and highly complex HS2-type things – and Heathrow is big and complex – it is rare to get nice surprises and much more common to get nasty ones.”

data.title: A 1960s bungalow transformed into a dream home
data.description:
When Elizabeth Taylor stumbled across an outdated 1960s bungalow hidden away in the West Sussex village where she grew up, she saw beyond its shabby façade. In its place came a vivid vision shaped by her travels along America’s East Coast, from New England to the Florida Keys, where she had fallen for the understated charm of clapboard houses. Painted white with dormer windows and wide porches, often with a swing, these rather romantic homes were typically set close to water. The only thing linking this property to those in America was its coastal setting, a small river running through its grounds and Elizabeth’s ability to imagine a complete transformation.

At the time, Elizabeth, a wedding photographer, was living nearby in a 1930s semi – her third renovation – and had no plans to move. But when she spotted the bungalow in an estate agent’s window, her curiosity was piqued. A viewing revealed a house hidden at the end of a private road, screened by towering leylandii and, inside, a range of Sixties features such as York-stone fireplaces, vinyl finishes, Artex ceilings and veneered wardrobes. It wasn’t the prettiest of houses, but it was detached, with a big garden. “It certainly wasn’t my dream home, but it had scope to go every which way, including up,” says Elizabeth. “I could picture it surrounded by azaleas, magnolias and camellias.” Six weeks later, in 2012, she had sold her home and moved in.

Decorating in stages

As Elizabeth had a young child and a limited budget, progress was gradual. “I started with the basics, just to make it liveable,” she says. A new bathroom was top of the list – particularly after her son discovered the walk-in bath would flood the room if it was opened mid-soak. Other rooms were stripped back and refreshed. “It smelt of the Sixties for about four years, until we finally got all the wallpaper and carpet out,” Elizabeth recalls.

While these early years didn’t bring about much change, they gave her time to think. “I’d previously renovated older homes with strong architectural features to steer design choices, but it was clear this project was going to be different,” she says. “I ended up concluding that I would just have to trust my instinct.”

In 2014, her now-husband Jamie Taylor, a builder and carpenter, entered the picture, and plans began to take shape. 

Reimagining the layout

The original layout needed rethinking. “It was completely disjointed,” says Elizabeth. She and Jamie worked with an architect to develop plans that balanced ambition with budget: “I thought having lots of options was a good thing, but it just sent me down endless Pinterest rabbit holes – unable to make decisions.” Rather than extending dramatically outwards, they eventually chose to build upwards to echo the American aesthetic that she so admired. The plans included loft and garage conversions, a modest extension and the addition of a veranda. Extending the front by just 1.5 metres to accommodate a hallway and porch proved transformative. “It was a small change, but it made a huge difference,” says Elizabeth.

Inside, the footprint remained similar, but awkward spaces were opened up. The cramped kitchen-diner, garage access and an odd outhouse were combined into one generous room, now the family’s day-to-day living space. “All those little nooks disappeared and suddenly the house made sense.”

Creating a budget kitchen with character

With the layout established, creating a kitchen with personality was central to Elizabeth’s vision, but staying within budget was a challenge. “Everything I loved was far too expensive,” she says. The solution came via eBay, where she found some old-school science cabinets. The cabinet doors had flat fronts but shaker-style backs, so they were simply reversed and rehung. Elizabeth then repainted each piece by hand, preserving the original lettering and brass fittings. “At one point I was using a tiny make-up brush to get into all the details,” she recalls. Glazed cabinets from the same find were fitted above. “Everyone thought I was mad putting in so many hours, but it turned out well and was a real bargain.”

Professionally fitted new quartz worktops added a touch of luxury, and they kept costs down elsewhere by reusing a sink from the old utility room. The space was designed to be sociable as well as practical, with a sofa, a salvaged island to gather around and a large dining table. Scaffold boards were lightly hand-sanded and varnished for flooring, and much of the furniture was sourced second-hand. “By using reclaimed materials and pre-loved furniture, we’ve created real character here,” says Elizabeth, who credits this approach with helping to shape and guide her overall design direction.

A resourceful approach

The former garage became a bedroom for Elizabeth’s son, Jack (now 21), with a vaulted ceiling and a mezzanine. The space between the kitchen and garage was enclosed to form a hallway and bathroom, creating a self-contained suite. Reclaimed scaffold boards were again used for flooring and stairs, while iron spindles from a car boot sale form the balustrade. “Scaffold boards have been a real hero material,” says Elizabeth. “They’re incredibly versatile and bring a warmth and texture that new materials just don’t have.”

The loft was also converted into a spacious bedroom with a dormer bathroom, and retained its dramatic vaulted ceiling.

Throughout, resourcefulness was key: “I wanted a high-end finish that reflected my taste, but we didn’t always have the budget,” says Elizabeth. Reclaimed materials, second-hand finds and Jamie’s craftsmanship kept costs manageable: only around 5 per cent of the materials and furnishings used in the project are new. The work was often done in stages, and sometimes they had to pause and wait for the time, money or motivation to continue.

Over the years, the total cost has reached around £250,000. “We never set a strict budget,” says Elizabeth. “We just had a vision and worked towards it whenever we could.” Part of the vision was to create a seamless connection between inside and out, achieved through the use of two complementary shades of cream across both the clapboard exterior and the interior.

Transforming the garden

Their attention then turned to the overgrown garden, beginning with the removal of the front hedging. “While it kept us completely private, it wasn’t the view we wanted,” says Elizabeth. “Taking it down was the best thing we could have done.” The change revealed the riverbank and mature trees, including elegant weeping willows, creating a stronger connection to the landscape.

This new outlook helped to define the vision for the rest of the garden. An old summer house was replaced with a purpose-built photography studio made from larch wood and reclaimed Crittall windows, while a greenhouse was created from salvaged materials. Landscaping introduced structure, with new paths, defined areas and a central patio. The result is a garden that feels open yet secluded.

“It finally feels like the garden belongs to the house,” says Elizabeth; and she feels she belongs there too. The project has become a deeply personal expression of everything she loves, and it seems a home made with love is a recipe for success.

See more on Instagram at @thetaylormadehouse

data.title: Dan Walker accused of sexism and bullying by former co-anchor
data.description:
Dan Walker has been accused of sexism, bullying and misogyny against a black woman in the workplace.

The former BBC broadcaster will face Claudia-Liza Vanderpuije, his former Channel 5 News co-anchor, at an employment tribunal hearing in London on April 20.

Ms Vanderpuije claims she was made redundant by Channel 5 after blowing the whistle on the “toxic culture of racism, sexism, misogyny and bullying [that] existed” in its newsroom. She alleges that Channel 5’s editorial content “reflected its racist culture”.

Walker, a married father of three, strenuously denies the allegations and reportedly has more than 50 character witness statements from colleagues, footballers and Olympians.

The Telegraph understands that an internal investigation into claims previously made by Ms Vanderpuije against the 49-year-old found there had been no misconduct.

An ITN spokesman said: “This claim, which is denied in full, will be addressed through the tribunal process.”

Mr Walker joined Ms Vanderpuije as a co-presenter in April 2022 after leaving the BBC. The pair hosted Channel 5 News for a year. Ms Vanderpuije left the programme in 2024.

Mr Walker is named as a respondent to Ms Vanderpuije’s allegations, along with ITN, which produces Channel 5 News, and Paramount, the station’s owner.

Ms Vanderpuije has hired Jonathan Coad, a media lawyer who previously represented Phillip Schofield.

Mr Coad told The Telegraph that the hearing was expected to last five weeks and would determine whether the claims should be upheld, rather than to decide on compensation.

He denied reports that his client had been seeking £13m in compensation, or a later £4m, and instead claimed that the respondents had made multiple offers of settlement which had been refused.

Mr Coad said: “Claudia-Liza claims that she was the victim of a sham redundancy because, as a whistleblower, she made protected disclosures alleging that a toxic culture of racism, sexism, misogyny and bullying existed in the Channel 5 newsroom, and that its editorial content reflected its racist culture.

“In her claim, she alleges that ITN and Channel 5 have tried to cover up the racist culture at Channel 5 News, which they have tolerated despite this breaching their statutory obligations under [the] Equality Act 2010 [and] the terms of their Ofcom licence.”

Presenter ‘stunned and very upset’

A source told The Mail on Sunday that Walker, a former BBC Breakfast and Football Focus host, “absolutely denies any sexism, misogyny, racism or bullying”, and that it had “been hanging over him for some years now”.

They added: “He will be at [the] London Central tribunal later this month. Nobody wants this, but he will be there to tell the judge the truth. There will be tons of witness statements which will talk to Dan’s character. He has been absolutely stunned with the amount of people willing to support him. He has been stunned and actually very upset to be accused of such things.”

In March 2024, it was revealed that Channel 5 had been investigating a multi-pronged complaint against Mr Walker, submitted by a colleague.

The next month, investigations said there had been “a lack of evidence to support allegations” and none of the claims was upheld.

Asked about the claims after he was exonerated, Mr Walker told The Telegraph: “That was fully investigated, and everything was completely incorrect, and I was entirely vindicated.

“So yeah, that’s not nice, but you hold on to what you know the truth is and ultimately the truth is the important thing. I think whenever you’re under scrutiny, it’s difficult. It’s important that the industry is scrutinised, but it’s also important that we hold on to what is true and what is right.”

Mr Walker has been approached for comment.

data.title: Aston Martin in legal fight with Chinese carmaker over wings logo
data.description:
Aston Martin is suing one of China’s biggest carmakers in an attempt to block it from using a wings logo similar to the badge it has used for almost a century.

The British carmaker filed a new intellectual property claim against Zhejiang Geely Holding Group in the UK this week, seeking to stop it from registering three new logos.

Aston Martin has argued that Geely’s new logos, which display the head of a horse on a circular badge at the centre of a pair of wings, are too similar to the feathered wings it uses as an emblem on its own luxury cars.

It marks the latest twist in a long-running battle between Aston Martin and Geely.

The row kicked off in 2022 when Geely first tried to register its three new logos with the UK’s Intellectual Property Office (IPO), for use by its London EV Company subsidiary, which makes electric-powered black cabs.

Aston Martin then attempted to block Geely from registering the trade marks through a legal challenge a year later by claiming that the Chinese firm’s logos could be mistaken for its own.

However, Aston Martin’s initial claim failed after the IPO tribunal ruled it unlikely that potential customers might be confused between Geely’s electric vehicles and the British firm’s luxury sports cars.

Aston Martin was subsequently ordered to pay £2,200 to Geely to cover its legal costs.

In its ruling, the tribunal added that similar wings logos are used by an array of leading car makers, including Bentley and Mini.

However, Aston Martin has since revived the legal battle by issuing a new claim in the Court of Appeal, seeking to overturn the 2023 ruling and block Geely from registering its wings logos.

Aston Martin first started using a wings-like logo in 1927, when it debuted a simple emblem showing the words Aston Martin in the shape of a pair of wings.

It then updated its logo five years later to a more complex design, inspired by winged scarab artefacts that were popular in Ancient Egypt. Aston Martin has used various iterations of that design ever since.

Geely owns a stake in Aston Martin

Zhejiang Geely Holding Group is a Chinese conglomerate which owns carmakers, including Volvo, Lotus, alongside Geely Auto, one of the biggest electric vehicle manufacturers in the world.

The Chinese firm is also one of Aston Martin’s biggest investors, after it acquired a 17pc stake in the luxury carmaker for £234m in 2023.

Geely started out manufacturing parts for fridges in 1986, before becoming a carmaker in the late 1990s. Geely is headquartered in Hangzhou, near Shanghai.

A Geely spokesman said: “This is a routine legal process in trademark confirmation and does not represent an unusual or hostile ‘legal battle’.

“While Geely Holding is a shareholder in Aston Martin, both companies operate independently. Trademark oppositions of this nature are common in the normal course of business and commercial brand protection.

“Geely remains committed to respectful and professional relations with Aston Martin.”

An Aston Martin spokesman said: “Aston Martin Lagonda does not comment on ongoing legal proceedings. Protecting our brand and intellectual property is a priority for the company, and we will continue to take appropriate steps to safeguard our trade marks in relevant markets.”

data.title: OpenAI blames Britain’s high energy prices as it halts flagship project
data.description:
The maker of ChatGPT has said Britain’s energy costs are too high as it suspended plans for data centre investments in the UK.

OpenAI said it was putting plans for a UK arm of its multibillion-dollar “Stargate” project on hold until power prices drop and conditions improve.

It marks a major setback to Sir Keir Starmer’s hopes of boosting growth through a data centre boom. The Government had previously trumpeted OpenAI’s plans for significant investment in Britain during Donald Trump’s state visit.

The $850bn (£634bn) American giant last year claimed that the Stargate UK project would strengthen the country’s “sovereign compute capabilities”. It said this would mean its AI models could “run on local computing power in the UK, for the UK”.

However, The Telegraph reported last week that the project had been delayed after it failed to hit a March deadline. OpenAI has cut spending plans globally.

The company said it continued to see “huge potential for the UK’s AI future”, adding: “London is home to our largest international research hub, and we support the Government’s ambition to be an AI leader.”

A spokesman said: “AI compute is foundational to that goal – we continue to explore Stargate UK and will move forward when the right conditions, such as regulation and the cost of energy, enable long-term infrastructure investment.

“In the meantime, we are investing in talent and expanding our local presence, while also delivering on the commitments under our [memorandum of understanding] with the Government to adopt frontier AI in UK public services.”

Britain suffers from the highest industrial electricity costs in the developed world, paying 63pc more than France and 27pc more than Germany, according to the International Energy Agency.

The average day-ahead price of electricity in Britain was about $104 per megawatt hour (MWh) over the past year, according to the agency. That compared with $74 per MWh in Japan, $64 in France and $43 in the United States.

Data centres, and particularly AI systems, are energy-intensive, making them vulnerable to soaring electricity costs.

Microsoft said last year that grid connection delays were putting investments at risk.

Stargate UK was announced last September as a project with British data centre company Nscale and chip giant Nvidia. OpenAI said it would involve a number of sites, starting with Cobalt Park, an existing data centre site in the North East.

The announcement led the Government to declare the North East one of its “AI growth zones”, which benefit from planning and power advantages.

George Osborne, the former chancellor, joined OpenAI last December to pursue data centre investments outside the US.

However, the company recently reduced spending commitments, saying it expected to spend around $600bn by 2030, compared to previous estimates of $1.4tn.

It is understood that discussions are ongoing about an investment.

A government spokesman said: “Our AI sector has attracted more than £100bn in private investment since the Government took office, with the sector growing 23 times faster than the wider economy last year. That is delivering the jobs and opportunities hard-working people deserve.

“Our focus is on continuing to create the right conditions for investment in the UK’s AI and data centre infrastructure. We are continuing to work with OpenAI and other leading AI companies to strengthen UK compute capacity.”

data.title: Hundreds of Hollywood stars oppose Paramount’s Warner Bros takeover
data.description:
Hundreds of Hollywood stars have come out against Paramount’s takeover of Warner Bros, claiming the deal to hand control of the studio to supporters of Donald Trump will hurt democracy.

Actors, including Jane Fonda, Glenn Close and Ben Stiller, have signed their names on an open letter expressing “unequivocal opposition” to the tie-up, arguing that competition is “essential for a healthy economy and a healthy democracy”.

The letter said the takeover “would further consolidate an already concentrated media landscape”, leading to “fewer opportunities for creators” and “less choice for audiences” worldwide.

It added: “We are deeply concerned by indications of support for this merger that prioritises the interests of a small group of powerful stakeholders over the broader public good.”

More than a thousand actors, screenwriters, producers, directors and publicists signed the letter, which called for Rob Bonta, the California attorney general, to block the deal.

Signatories included: Joaquin Phoenix, star of Joker; Twilight actress Kristen Stewart; US Traitors host Alan Cumming; Bryan Cranston, Breaking Bad’s lead actor; and Slow Horses star Kristin Scott Thomas.

Directors including David Fincher, known for Fight Club and Gone Girl, and Denis Villeneuve, who is behind the Dune franchise, also signed their names on it.

Paramount agreed to buy Warner Bros for $111bn (£82bn) in February after outbidding Netflix, which had earlier struck a deal to pay $82bn for the film studio.

The takeover has fuelled fears that the billionaire Ellison family will be handed outsized control over American media. Paramount has already taken control of CBS News and Larry Ellison played a central role in the takeover of TikTok’s US operations.

Mr Ellison and his son David Ellison, who runs Paramount, are both close to Mr Trump.

RedBird Capital, the US private equity fund that mounted a takeover bid for The Telegraph and is Paramount’s second-largest shareholder, is also backing the bid.

Ms Fonda said: “A Warner Bros Discovery-Paramount merger would be one of the most destructive threats to free speech and creative expression in our history.

“This deal would place unprecedented power in the hands of a single corporation that already appears to have proven itself willing to sacrifice integrity for political favour.”

Paramount took control of CBS News in an $8bn deal last year and has installed Bari Weiss, the founder of “anti-woke” news site The Free Press, as its editor-in-chief. Mr Ellison’s Oracle Corporation was also part of the consortium that acquired TikTok’s operations.

Growing concerns in Hollywood

Senator Elizabeth Warren, a senior Democrat for Massachusetts, said in February: “A handful of Trump-aligned billionaires are trying to seize control of what you watch and charge you whatever price they want.”

The open letter said: “Increasingly, a small number of powerful entities determine what gets made – and on what terms – leaving creators and independent businesses with fewer viable paths to sustain their work.”

Mr Ellison, the 81-year-old founder of Oracle Corporation, is a long-time friend and supporter of Trump and has helped fund his political campaigns.

As well as raising concerns about free speech, the Hollywood stars who put their names to the letter warned the Warner Bros’ takeover would endanger “the professional lives of the tens of thousands of workers”.

The letter said: “Our industry is already under severe strain, in large part due to prior waves of consolidation. We have witnessed a steep decline in the number of films produced and released, alongside a narrowing of the kinds of stories that are financed and distributed.”

The UK’s competition watchdog separately said on Monday that it would investigate Paramount’s takeover of Warner Bros.

A spokesman for the Competition and Markets Authority said: “The film and TV industries contribute billions to our economy, so it’s important we assess whether deals between studios may harm competition.”

A Paramount spokesman said: “Paramount remains deeply committed to talent, and this merger strengthens both consumer choice and competition, creating greater opportunities for creators, audiences and the communities they live and work in.”

Warner Bros was contacted for comment.
