data.title: GB News accuses Labour MP of spreading populism ‘conspiracy theory’
data.description:
The chairman of GB News has accused a senior Labour MP of peddling a conspiracy theory over claims the channel is a mouthpiece for populism.

Alan McCormick, a partner at GB News co-owner Legatum, has criticised Liam Byrne after he claimed the broadcaster’s investors were “volatility traders” hoping to cash in on chaos in Britain.

Writing in The Telegraph, Mr McCormick said the claim “verges on conspiracy theory”, adding: “It demonstrates an ignorance about how the media works.”

Mr Byrne, who chairs the House of Commons’s business and trade committee, has accused GB News’s backers of seeking to promote populist politicians to unleash market chaos they can profit from.

The Labour MP commissioned research for his book, Why Populists are Winning and How to Beat Them, that found more than £170m had been handed to media organisations, politicians and think tanks aligned with the populist Right over the last five years.

In a robust rebuttal, Mr McCormick dismissed claims of political bias at GB News as “nonsense” and insisted the channel provided a platform for a more diverse array of opinions than any of its rivals.

He wrote: “The money invested in GB News by its investors has also been wildly mischaracterised in various places as being politically motivated. This is simply not the case.

“GB News was founded to surface the topics the British people want to talk about and the ideas that work, taking it off the political continuum, and not aligned with any political party.”

In response, Mr Byrne told The Telegraph: “Well, he would say that, wouldn’t he?”

He added: “Ofcom know exactly what they’re supposed to be doing to impose some proper rules at GB News – they need to get on and do it.”

It marks a rare intervention by Mr McCormick, who was appointed as chairman of GB News in 2022 following the abrupt departure of founder Andrew Neil. Dubai-based hedge fund Legatum co-owns the channel alongside the hedge fund tycoon Sir Paul Marshall.

The investors have pumped more than £140m into the loss-making channel since its inception five years ago. The latest accounts for the year to March 2025 show they injected a further £17.7m as losses narrowed to £22m.

GB News has repeatedly surpassed the BBC News channel and Sky News for audience numbers while revenues have grown as the channel shrugged off an advertising boycott that dogged its early years.

But the broadcaster has been embroiled in repeated controversies over impartiality, including its use of politicians such as Nigel Farage and Sir Jacob Rees-Mogg as presenters.

The channel was fined £100,000 by regulator Ofcom in 2024 for breaking impartiality rules in a programme featuring Rishi Sunak, who was the prime minister at the time.

It has also previously been censured for airing conspiracy theories linked to Covid-19 vaccines.

However, GB News scored a major victory over the regulator last year, when the High Court overturned a ruling that the channel had breached the broadcasting code in a programme presented by Sir Jacob.

Ofcom subsequently planned to tighten its rules to block politicians from acting as presenters on any show on TV or radio, but dropped the proposals in the face of fierce opposition from GB News and Rupert Murdoch’s News UK.

The picture Liam Byrne paints verges on conspiracy theory

By Alan McCormick, the chairman of GB News

Why would anyone invest in a broadcast news channel today? In our age of streaming and our attention-based economy, combined with declining television consumption, it may not seem like a growth industry. What’s more, the “media mogul” is hardly the most universally loved of stock characters.

Yet that is where Legatum, the private investment firm in which I am a partner, happily finds itself in 2026. Alongside Sir Paul Marshall, we invested in All Perspectives Ltd, the parent company of GB News, in 2021 when the channel launched.

The main reason we invested is very simple. It’s the same reason that investors back almost any project: we believed it would be a sound commercial enterprise. And more, we believed it would meet an audience need that was unmet by the incumbent providers.

Five years later, that judgment has been thoroughly vindicated – GB News is Britain’s number-one news channel.

As of March 2026, it has beaten both the BBC and Sky in seven of the last eight months, both in average audience and audience share.

In February, it had an average audience of 99,000 viewers, up 13pc on the previous month and far surpassing the BBC’s 78,400. Revenues have increased 65pc in the last 12 months, and while the company has been loss-making, we are patient and confident that in time the business will prosper.

Far from making the most of what remains of a declining industry, GB News is at the cutting edge of media technology. GB News is a technology platform, having pioneered hardware that is now being used by the rest of the industry. Visit one of our studios and you will find a slick, automated operation which embraces new tech and AI unencumbered by legacy broadcast hardware and practices.

Why did we believe that GB News would prove to be a sound investment? There is a clue in the name of the channel’s parent company: All Perspectives. To us, it was evident that a major gap existed in the media marketplace for voices that were routinely ignored by legacy broadcasters – not fringe voices, but those of normal communities all across Britain.

The decline of broadcast media cannot be solely blamed on the rise of the internet and streaming. It is also a product of people switching off because the stories which matter to them have been ignored by broadcasters for a very long time. Viewers simply didn’t want what the established channels were selling.

This judgement has been vindicated. Viewers are tuning in to GB News in droves, largely because they finally hear their perspectives being taken seriously and given airtime. And yet they are also exposed to genuine debate, making nonsense of suggestions that the channel is a mouthpiece for a particular political agenda.

GB News guests and panels showcase far more diversity of opinion than its competitors. Rather than the carefully choreographed and contained “discussions” found on other channels, GB News puts those with opposite views into conversation with one another, and allows viewers to decide for themselves.

Some people have their own ideas about why we would make such an investment. In the course of promoting his new book on so-called populism, Liam Byrne MP has suggested that we, investors in GB News, are “volatility traders”, planning to disrupt Britain in order to profit from future chaos by making big bets on the markets.

Investors certainly do plenty of sophisticated, long-term thinking, but the picture Mr Byrne paints verges on conspiracy theory. It demonstrates an ignorance about how the media works. While there are certainly easier and speedier ways to drive returns, we view this as a very significant, long-term investment in British journalism.

The money invested in GB News has also been wildly mischaracterised in various places as being politically motivated. This is simply not the case. GB News was founded to surface the topics the British people want to talk about and the ideas that work, taking it off the political continuum, and not aligning with any party.

The investors behind GB News have always been guided by the value of competition and free enterprise, which is a fundamental British ideal. That applies to our investment in a media company as much as any other project. We sincerely value an open media landscape, one in which genuinely diverse views compete on a level playing field.

News channels should be free to present their very best broadcasting, to win viewers to their conclusions, and to generate revenue whilst they do so.

Such a liberated media landscape is what many critics of GB News say they want too. If they are as sincere about that as we are, then GB News’ success should be welcomed by its competitors and critics as much as by its rapidly growing audience.

data.title: Britain faces food shortages because of Iran war
data.description:
Britain is facing a summer of food shortages because of the Iran war. A shortage of carbon dioxide could mean chicken, pork and fizzy drinks are in short supply if the conflict continues, secret government contingency planning for a “worst-case scenario” reveals.

Senior officials from departments including No 10, the Treasury, and the Ministry of Defence rehearsed scenarios examining the potential impact of the war on British industry in an event codenamed Exercise Turnstone.

The contingency plans, seen by The Times, were based on a scenario where the Strait of Hormuz has not reopened and no deal to end the war has been reached by mid-June. Farmers were warned of potential disruption and hospitality businesses, already squeezed by Labour’s tax rises, could be hit first by a shortage of CO2.

This is because CO2 is used in the process of slaughtering pigs and chickens and making drinks fizzy. It also improves the shelf life of foods such as salads, packaged meats and cakes. 

Officials also believe a collapse in CO2 supplies could endanger lives by making dry ice used to cool blood supplies, organs and vaccines scarcer. Under a reasonable worst-case scenario, CO2 supplies would fall to just 18 per cent of current levels, and factories would be ordered to stop other manufacturing to boost supplies by up to 100 per cent.

Emergency legislation to compel factories to co-operate has been discussed, with suppliers being compensated for stopping production of their main products.

The worst-case scenario involved an important UK plant suffering a mechanical error, while high gas costs led to lower production of ammonia and fertiliser, which produces CO2 as a by-product across Europe.

Critical food shortages are not expected, but shoppers could see fewer variety of goods on supermarket shelves.

Britain is expected to be hit hard by the economic fallout from the war.

On Wednesday, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) forecast that Britain is expected to suffer the biggest hit to growth of any G7 economy.

In a triple blow for Rachel Reeves, the IMF cut its UK growth forecasts for this year and next, warned of the highest inflation in the G7 and predicted one of the smallest increases in living standards in the world.

Higher fuel prices and energy bills are forecast to cost households £480 this year, according to the Resolution Foundation, with middle-class families being hit hardest.

On Thursday, Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed planning for food shortages was under way and said Sir Keir Starmer was “leading from the front”.

Mr Kyle told Times Radio: “Of course we don’t comment on leaks. But this now is out there. But I want to reassure people that we have been acting since the very start of this conflict.”

He added: “We are looking at a variety of scenarios... When people read what is a leak and it is difficult for me because these leaks are very unhelpful, but when people read it they need to be reassured that we are doing this planning and we are doing this kind of scenario planning.

“I can tell you because I’m in these meetings that the Prime Minister has been there since the start of this war and he is going through and driving deep dives into resilience in lots of areas in our economy. You have a Government that is acting with creativity and boldness.”

Mr Kyle insisted food shortages were currently “not a concern” and that he would be “upfront” with the public if that were to change.

A government spokesman said: “We took decisive action last month to shore up the UK’s critical supplies of CO2 by temporarily restarting the Ensus bioethanol plant in Teesside and are continuing to work closely with business groups to tackle the impacts of events in the Middle East.

“Reasonable worst-case scenarios are a planning tool used by experts and are not a prediction of future events.”

data.title: Channel 4 programme chief quits amid shake-up at broadcaster
data.description:
Channel 4’s veteran head of programmes is to step down amid a leadership overhaul at the youth-focused broadcaster.

Ian Katz, the chief content officer, will leave in the autumn after nearly nine years in the role, making him the longest-serving programme boss in the channel’s history.

Mr Katz, a former BBC Newsnight editor and senior journalist at The Guardian, joined Channel 4 in 2018. He is credited with bringing shows such as Taskmaster to the channel, while he also oversaw the Russell T Davies drama It’s A Sin and documentaries including Russell Brand: In Plain Sight.

The 58-year-old was also responsible for driving Channel 4’s expansion on YouTube as the broadcaster tries to navigate a decline in traditional TV viewing and exodus of young audiences to streaming rivals.

However, Channel 4 has faced criticism in recent years that it has lost its edge and now relies too heavily on old formats such as Gogglebox and The Great British Bake Off. The channel was launched under Margaret Thatcher in 1982 with a remit to make challenging and provocative shows.

Mr Katz’s departure comes a month after Priya Dogra took over as chief executive of Channel 4. Ms Dogra was selected to replace Alex Mahon, who left last year after successfully fending off attempts to privatise the broadcaster.

The former Sky and Warner Bros executive will now seek to shape her new leadership team after a period of uncertainty for Channel 4, which lost both its chairman and chief executive last year.

Sir Ian Cheshire, the previous chairman, stood down at the end of his term last April and was appointed this month as the new chairman of regulator Ofcom.

Jonathan Allan, Channel 4’s former chief operating officer, who was in the running for the top job, also stepped down earlier this year.

Ms Dogra said: “Ian has been an outstanding creative leader for Channel 4 over nearly nine years – the channel’s longest-serving head of programming.

“He has overseen an era of creative renewal, delivering bold and distinctive public service programming with intellectual rigour, good humour and an unmistakably Channel 4 glint in the eye.”

The leadership shake-up comes as Channel 4 seeks to carve out its role in the streaming era. The broadcaster is spending less on making programmes as it grapples with declining advertising revenues from its traditional TV business.

Channel 4 has been striking closer partnerships with its rivals as public service broadcasters seek to build scale to fight back against the likes of Netflix. But it is also contending with a wider reshaping of the TV landscape, with Sky locked in talks to buy ITV’s broadcasting business.

Mr Katz said: “It’s been a privilege beyond words to lead Channel 4’s talented and passionate commissioning team through such a transformative period.

“I’m incredibly proud of what we’ve achieved, from pioneering the evolution of a commercial public service broadcaster into a digital streaming business to backing groundbreaking programmes and talent that have brought a bit of joy to audiences’ lives.

“Above all, I’m proud that Channel 4 has continued to be as disruptive and impertinent as ever, asking the questions and covering the stories that other broadcasters and streamers won’t.”

Mr Katz will remain in his role until October to commission and oversee the channel’s slate of programming for 2026.

data.title: Britain has record taxes yet Labour’s mismanagement has left us broke
data.description:
The Governor of the Bank of England has warned that the world is facing a “very big energy shock” that will push up prices.

A statement of the bleeding obvious, as Basil Fawlty might say? Perhaps, but it serves to highlight just how insufficient the response has been to the economic fallout from Donald Trump’s misadventures in the Middle East.

The Government is so far out of its depth that armbands and a rubber ring may have to become compulsory items at future Cabinet meetings.

The latest iteration of the energy support scheme for industry is merely further evidence that ministers either still don’t get it or aren’t willing to provide meaningful support.

The decision to rethink the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which provides a 25pc cut in bills for so-called energy-intensive users, was supposed to demonstrate that someone in Government finally had grasped the severity of the situation facing manufacturers.

Instead, the opposite is true. Sure, expanding the breadth of it to include over 10,000 factories rather than the 7,000 first proposed is meaningful, but not if support isn’t brought forward. For reasons no one in power has been able to provide a compelling explanation for, those that qualify for relief still won’t be able to access it until April 2027.

The mind truly boggles. Without real help now, Britain risks losing some of the most critical parts of the supply chain forever, undermining our already fragile national sovereignty further and making us even more reliant on volatile foreign suppliers for materials that underpin scores of vital products.

The American owner of the UK’s last producer of a critical chemical called aniline has warned that it would close the site in Wilton, Teesside, if energy prices remained at their current levels for the next three months.

It means the amended BICS scheme, which Peter Kyle wasted little time in championing on national radio on Thursday, won’t make the slightest jot of difference to the fate of at least one major factory. The future of many more will be hanging in the balance.

The Business Secretary had the audacity to claim the changes to BICS showed Labour was “a government of action” that would “always do what’s needed” in times of global instability. Twaddle.

The only sensible conclusion is that Labour’s woeful mismanagement of the economy, under which welfare is being repeatedly prioritised over everything else including even our national security, has rendered Britain so broke that it could not afford to respond in times of genuine crisis.

This, of course, shouldn’t be the case. As the International Monetary Fund (IMF) pointed out this week, Rachel Reeves has raised taxes at the fastest pace in the world.

The Chancellor’s repeated raids on households and businesses would push the UK’s tax burden to a peacetime high of 42pc of GDP by the start of the next decade, the IMF’s figures showed. This equates to an extra £130bn of taxes since Labour took power, or a staggering £4,500 more per household.

But because of this Government’s obsession with welfare and the NHS, there is no money for energy or defence – or much else, for that matter. As Anna Leach, the chief economist at the Institute of Directors, points out, the UK is “uniquely vulnerable” to events in the Middle East because of “financing conditions, high initial starting point for inflation, uncompetitive energy costs and low fiscal space”.

It explains why the IMF expects growth in Britain this year to be slower, and inflation higher than any other major advanced economy.

The problem is spending, or rather where that spending is being directed. The intervention of Lord Robertson – a Labour grandee, former secretary-general of Nato, as well as the author of the Government’s recent strategic defence review – is telling.

Perhaps it will prove to be decisive in ensuring that some of the ever-expanding welfare budget is redirected to other departments. When serious figures like Robertson are starting to openly question the benefits bill, it is surely time for a complete rethink of where resources are being channelled. As he points out, Britain’s welfare budget is now five times the amount we spend on defence at a time when national security has never been more vital.

Perhaps naive ministers have allowed the latest GDP figures to fool them into thinking the economy could weather the incoming storm.

No doubt February’s numbers showing a 0.5pc rise in GDP are a pleasant surprise, but any growth had almost certainly already been extinguished by events in the Middle East – Kyle himself acknowledged this.

It is why the failure to provide meaningful and immediate support for heavy industry makes no sense. Left to fend for themselves in the face of even higher energy and raw material costs than they were already grappling with thanks to the war in Ukraine, and endless government-imposed green levies, manufacturers will be forced to curtail investment, cut back on hiring, and possibly even reduce output. Under those circumstances, growth becomes little more than an aspiration.

Yet, at the same time, ministers are drawing up plans to invoke emergency legislation to force many of those very same factories to double CO2 production to avoid shortages that will ripple through the economy. Forget a fizzy drinks crisis, a CO2 scarcity could quickly become a threat to life by affecting supplies of vaccines, blood and vital medicines.

Given this, you would think ministers would be bending over backwards to provide help. Instead, the best they could come up with was a rehashed scheme that still wouldn’t be available for another year – proof that the disconnect at the top has reached epic new proportions.

On Thursday, Sir Keir Starmer told tech bosses hauled into No 10 to discuss child safety: “The status quo simply cannot be allowed to stand.” With local elections around the corner, the same could be said of Labour’s time in power.

data.title: Founder of Chinese property giant pleads guilty to fraud
data.description:
The founder of the failed Chinese property giant Evergrande is facing the threat of life in prison after pleading guilty to fraud and bribery.

Hui Ka Yan, also known as Xu Jiayin and once one of China’s richest men with a fortune of $45bn (£36bn), made the plea during a court hearing in Shenzhen earlier this week.

It marks a significant fall from grace for the businessman, who turned Evergrande into China’s biggest property developer before it collapsed under the weight of huge debts in 2021.

Evergrande’s unravelling has led to a raft of claims against Hui, including embezzlement of assets and illegal fundraising. The offences carry a maximum penalty of life imprisonment and confiscation of property.

The court in Shenzhen said it would announce its verdict on the case at a later date, claiming on Tuesday that Hui had “pleaded guilty and expressed remorse” during proceedings.

His trial took place after he was detained in China in Sept 2023 on suspicion of committing crimes. Hui, a former steelworker, has not been seen publicly since.

At its peak, Evergrande had a stock market valuation of more than £37bn, working on around 1,300 projects in 280 cities across China.

However, large debts crippled the company in 2021, sending the company’s value plummeting and sparking China’s property downturn, which has weighed on the economy for years.

The China Securities Regulatory Commission found in 2024 that Evergrande had inflated earnings and committed securities fraud.

The commission found that revenues were overstated by more than 560bn yuan (£60.6bn) by recognising sales in advance.

It fined Hui 47m yuan (£5.1m) and handed him a lifetime ban from participating in China’s stock markets.

The indebted property giant was fined 4.18bn yuan (£451.4m) before being ordered to liquidate by a Hong Kong court in 2024. The company was removed from the Hong Kong stock exchange last year.

The collapse of the company has fuelled a property crisis across China, as home sales slowed and construction companies scaled back developments.

Beijing has sought to prop up the property market by providing incentives to prospective home buyers.

However, the economy has since been dealt a fresh blow by the Iran war, straining China’s energy supplies and piling pressure on the country’s manufacturing sector.

data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”

data.title: How Sadiq Khan made London into a taxi driver’s worst nightmare
data.description:
It should be a straightforward journey that needn’t take too long – Liverpool Street to London Bridge station, two areas heavily populated with white-collar workers heading from the commuter belts into the capital for their careers.

However, those opting to drive the short one-and-a-half miles find themselves forced to go a more roundabout way – through Whitechapel, in order to avoid the bus gate at Bishopsgate, which prevents motorists (yet allows buses and bikes) from accessing the road.

So, instead of taking around five minutes in the car, it takes between 20 and 30 minutes. Realistically, it would be quicker for drivers to swap four wheels for two legs.

Bishopsgate is just one of the many roads in the capital that have become increasingly unwelcoming to motorists, with a recent survey conducted by TomTom, the satnav company, finding that London is the slowest-driving capital city in the world.

It takes an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to travel one measly kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of the city. The analysis also found drivers lost an average of 141 hours stuck in rush hour traffic in 2025.

This glacial pace is annoying when you are trying to get somewhere in a hurry, to attend an important meeting, perhaps, or to make it to the theatre before curtain call. However, for the black cab drivers of London, increasingly congested, slow and difficult-to-navigate roads are leading to a significant loss of earnings, and even putting their livelihoods – and the sight of the London icon – at risk of extinction.

As of late 2025, the number of licensed drivers had fallen to around 14,800, down from more than 22,500 a decade earlier.

For Steve Cox, who became a black cab driver in 2022, navigating the streets of London has become increasingly difficult. He cites low-traffic neighbourhoods (LTNs) – areas where roads are closed to traffic to allow cyclists and pedestrians to take priority – as one of the main contributors to the capital’s slow roads.

“What used to be a 15-minute journey can now take you up to an hour,” the 54-year-old explains. “Tottenham Court Road is severely restricted for motorists between 8am and 7pm, which are some of your peak hours as a black cab.

“If you want to go from one side of Tottenham Court Road to the other to drop a passenger, we have to do two different squares and go round several back roads. It’s just ridiculous.

“It’s another 15 to 20 minutes on the journey time, and probably another £15-£20 for the customer. I’m embarrassed to charge them. It’s unfair and leads to complaints that aren’t down to me – and results in fewer people trying to flag us down.”

While LTNs were initially implemented in London in the 1970s (although under a different name) to filter traffic in the Hackney area, they were implemented en masse in March 2020 under Mayor of London Sir Sadiq Khan’s plans to improve road safety, decrease air and noise pollution, and encourage “active travel” such as walking and cycling.

Now, it is thought four per cent of London’s nine-million-strong population lives in an LTN.

These have made navigating around the capital increasingly problematic; approximately 95 per cent of black cab drivers report that LTNs and poor road conditions make picking up and dropping off passengers difficult.

Cabbies also cite London’s new speed restrictions as a reason why the capital often remains at a standstill. According to Transport for London (TfL), more than half of London’s roads now have a 20mph limit. This includes all roads within London’s Congestion Charge zone, as well as major roads, such as the A107 corridor and the White Hart Roundabout.

While this was implemented to try and cut deaths and serious injuries, City Hall was previously told this change was “devastating to the taxi industry” by Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association in 2024.

“I understand the need for 20mph zones around schools,” says Frank O’Beirne, who has been a black cab driver for 14 years. “However, places like Park Lane, which have been reduced from three lanes of traffic to just one in order for there to be a bus and cycling lane, is just madness – especially now we’re stuck only being able to go 20mph there.”

The 50-year-old adds: “While our meters do go up in traffic, it’s only incremental – we need to be moving to actually have decent fares. Customers get so fed up in traffic they just jump out.”

Sadiq Khan’s green crusade for London has reached a nadir with the highly criticised and controversial ultra-low emission zone (Ulez). Since 2018, all newly-licenced black cabs need to be “zero-emission capable”, while the maximum operating age for older, more polluting diesel taxis has been reduced from 15 years to 12.

Cox says some older cabbies have been forced into an early retirement. “I know men in their 60s who have had their cab for 10 years and just didn’t see the point of carrying on investing so much money in a new car, and have gone and got jobs in Tesco instead.

“That and the stress of trying to earn enough money to keep you afloat is enough to push someone into another job. I don’t blame people for saying: ‘I don’t need this anymore.’”

It’s of little surprise, then, that a quarter of all black cabs on the roads before the pandemic are no longer in operation. Renting a traditional black cab, or Hackney carriage, is not cheap. Cox pays £360 a week to rent his vehicle, while others pay £400 and beyond. That doesn’t include the additional outgoings for cabbies, such as the mandatory two MOTs a year; while cab drivers don’t pay this, it does result in a loss of earnings as you can’t work while your vehicle is being serviced.

Cox says he needs to earn more than £3,000 a month just to break even on the investments he’s made to become a black cab driver. “It’s a stressful trade,” he says. “It’s made even more stressful by sudden roadworks, or ongoing protests by groups such as Palestine Action blocking roads. I can lose up to £300 a day in earnings when protests take place.”

Those still working as black cab drivers are now finding alternative methods to keep themselves afloat. O’Beirne works as a tour guide during slow periods. “I prefer doing out-of-town stuff now, because London just drives me mad,” he says. “Places like Tottenham Court Road, Mayfair, the City are just no-go areas. It’s a nightmare to navigate.

“Corporate gigs tend to pay better – I get a lot of American customers, who tend to be big tippers. But doing these gigs means you give up some freedom of setting your own hours, which is partly the beauty of doing this job.”

So, what could help London’s black cab drivers stay on the roads? For Cox, cutting, or at least reducing, some of the restrictions of LTNs on Hackney carriages would help with picking up and dropping off customers.

“If we really want to ease congestion, we could put a cap on private-hire drivers,” he adds. “They can operate without any restriction on the size of their fleet. I’ve heard of private-hire drivers getting licensed in Wolverhampton and then driving down here to get more work.”

Currently, private-hire vehicles vastly outstrip black cabs; TfL estimates more than 106,000 of these cabs are on the road.

Meanwhile, Steve Kenton, who has been a cabbie for 35 years, believes City Hall should listen more to black cab drivers. “We have some wonderful and intelligent people who choose to become black cab drivers,” he says. “We spend three to four years perfecting the Knowledge of London so we know the proper routes around the city. We attract individuals from every demographic. Our views and concerns deserve to be treated with more respect.”

“Fundamentally, if our wheels aren’t moving, we aren’t making money,” O’Beirne agrees. “We’re the only fully accessible transport network in London. Not everyone can ride a bike.”

data.title: Any company that adopts surge pricing deserves to get wiped out
data.description:
A sandwich at Pret might cost extra if it is a busy lunchtime. A pint might be another 50p at Wetherspoons if there is a crush at the bar, while the strawberries could double in price at Tesco a few hours before the Wimbledon final.

According to a report from the Bank of England this week, a third of companies are planning to experiment with surge pricing this year, charging more for their products at peak times.

In a free market, companies can adopt any pricing model they think works for them. And yet surge pricing is a terrible idea. Customers want clear, consistent and preferably good-value pricing – and businesses that forget that will deserve to be wiped out by their rivals very quickly.

We have all become far too familiar with surge pricing already. We already know that four tickets to a Greek island with Ryanair – on the first day of the school holidays – will cost us a lot more than the same flight would on a Tuesday night in February.

Likewise, we know that an Uber is going to cost us a lot more when it is pouring with rain, and concert tickets may well quadruple in price if everyone wants to book the same gig. But it could be about to become a lot more common.

The Bank of England says 31pc of companies plan to introduce what it politely termed “market responsive pricing tools” this year, up from 21pc 12 months ago. It gets worse. The supermarkets may soon have the technology to change prices minute by minute, and AI bots may even be able to analyse customer data so precisely that prices can be varied not just by time, but by person as well.

In other words, if you really need a giant packet of Kettle chips and a couple of beers before settling down to watch the football – and who doesn’t – then the shop can charge you extra for them.

We can all understand why companies are experimenting with new pricing models. Technology has made it far easier to adjust prices in real time. It started with the apps, because while an old-fashioned taxi meter charged a fixed price per mile, Uber simply found a price that matched supply and demand, and that was different according to the time and place.

But now it is possible to vary prices all the time, and for almost every business. Restaurants and bars now have menus that have to be downloaded onto your phone, instead of being expensively reprinted every time the price goes up, while even the supermarkets increasingly have electronic displays instead of old-fashioned price tags.

Surge pricing allows the seller to maximise their profits, charging what the customer will bear, instead of sticking to a fixed price when they could have got more. And, in fairness, it allows companies to compensate for one-off factors, such as the huge rise in energy prices we have witnessed over the last few weeks without selling at a loss, which would otherwise be very hard.

The trouble is, while it might make sense occasionally, it is a terrible idea once the majority of companies attempt it.

There are three big problems. First, it is exhausting for consumers. For most people, as soon as they know that the pricing is dynamic, there is a temptation to hunt around for the best deal. Wait for 10 minutes and the same product might cost 10pc less. It is time consuming, and turns every purchase into a huge chore.

Next, no one wants to feel ripped off. You might be willing to pay more for a product or service than the next person, perhaps because you have more money, or you really need it, but it is always frustrating to feel you have paid more than you had to.

Finally it will prove inflationary. It is hard to escape the suspicion that, while there are occasionally bargains, at least if you don’t mind flying to Corfu via Dusseldorf at three in the morning, on the whole prices only surge upwards. Indeed, it is not hard to understand why the Bank of England is getting worried. There used to be a certain stickiness to prices, because it was hard work to change all the labels.

But now it can be done instantaneously, and at zero cost. It makes it a lot harder to keep prices under control, or even to measure them accurately given that they might start changing at bewildering speed. The traditional inflation statistics will become meaningless if they no longer reflect the prices being charged on a day-to-day basis.

Surge pricing might make sense on a McKinsey powerpoint presentation, but out in the real world it will prove a catastrophe for companies that start using it more than they absolutely have to.

What customers want is fair, consistent prices. We will probably hear lots of calls for government inquiries, regulations and controls, such as the outcry over the cost of Oasis tickets last year. But what we really need to do is to make sure there is plenty of competition, and that, as customers, we avoid businesses that try to make their prices dynamic.

Companies can introduce surge pricing if they want to. It is a free market and everyone can compete anyway they want to. There may even be a few oddballs out there who might enjoy an AI bot analysing their consumer preferences and charging them the highest price it thinks it can get away with.

But it won’t work for most mainstream businesses – and any companies that try it deserve to get wiped out by rivals who charge a fair price, and then stick with it, regardless of the weather or the time of day.

data.title: Asda considers vending machines to fight shoplifting
data.description:
Asda is drawing up proposals to put perfume and batteries into vending machines to stop criminals stealing them from shelves as Britain grapples with a shoplifting epidemic.

The supermarket is holding talks about installing vending machines across its stores for products that are regularly targeted by criminals.

This includes potentially placing batteries, razor blades and perfumes into these machines, taking them off the shelves so that customers would have to pay before accessing them. These items tend to be at higher risk of being stolen as they are easier for criminal gangs to sell on the black market.

The discussions follow a trial of a vending machine in an Asda store in Ashton-under-Lyne, Greater Manchester, where it uses the technology to sell vapes and cigarettes.

The system involves customers choosing what they want on a screen and receiving a ticket that they take to tills to pay for. They then receive a second ticket which they can take to a vending machine that dispenses the product they want.

Speaking to staff at a meeting late last month, Asda bosses said they were looking at options for vending machines for “high-theft items”.

No decisions have been made yet on which items would be included in vending machines and a rollout is not believed to be imminent.

Supermarkets are racing to find ways to stop criminals from targeting their stores without putting staff at risk.

Marks & Spencer last weekend warned that crime was getting worse rather than better.

Writing in The Telegraph, Thinus Keeve, the company’s retail chief, said attacks were becoming “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive” across Britain’s high streets.

He said recent incidents included gangs forcing open locked cabinets and stripping shelves, as well as staff being headbutted and having ammonia thrown in their face.

The latest crime report compiled by the British Retail Consortium (BRC) found that there were 5.5 million recorded shoplifting incidents last year, costing retailers nearly £400m. However, the BRC said the total number of incidents was likely to be higher as supermarkets were not always recording or aware of attacks.

M&S executives recently wrote to both the Mayor of London and the Home Secretary to demand they do more to tackle shoplifting and crime.

Mobs of youths have ran riot in Clapham, south London, over the past month, targeting an M&S store and lighting fires.

Supermarkets have spent more than £5bn in the last five years to improve security measures, investing in more CCTV and security staff at doorways.

However, retailers are becoming increasingly concerned about staff getting involved to prevent attacks.

It emerged last week that Waitrose had dismissed an employee after he attempted to stop a shoplifter stealing a bag of Easter eggs. Waitrose said at the time that the “correct process is being followed, which includes a standard appeals procedure”.

Other British supermarkets have also trialled vending-machine technology to address the issue, including Morrisons and Sainsbury’s.

It is more widely used across the rest of Europe – particularly in Norway and Sweden – to stock products including medication and phone cards.

A spokesman for Asda said: “We continually explore new ideas which may help to improve our customers’ and colleagues’ experiences whilst in our stores. However, there are no immediate plans to install new vending machines for any products in our stores whilst a trial in our Ashton store is ongoing.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Bank to quit Britain in protest against ‘unfair’ £750m car finance bill
data.description:
A bank has said it will quit the UK in protest after an “unfair” scheme that will see its car finance compensation payouts soar to £750m.

FirstRand, which owns roughly a tenth of the UK car finance sector through MotoNovo, has vowed to abandon its UK operations as result of the City watchdog’s mass redress scheme for mis-sold car loans.

In a notice to shareholders, the South African bank said it would leave its motor finance operations in Britain because it no longer has the “risk appetite” for the UK.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) finalised details of the scheme at the end of March, which FirstRand said were “disproportionate and unfair”.

The terms of the scheme mean FirstRand now expects its compensation bill to hit £750m, up from its previous estimate of £510m.

The firm’s announcement shows how the fallout from the car finance scandal is rocking the sector as millions of borrowers prepare to claw back compensation.

Around 12 million drivers will be entitled for an average payout of £829 under the FCA’s scheme, which will cost the car finance industry as much as £9.1bn.

This compensation is for customers who were sold car finance agreements in which dealers received a fee from lenders based on the interest rate charged on the loans.

The FCA, which banned these agreements in 2021, said these fees were rarely disclosed to customers and provided an incentive for dealers to charge higher interest rates than necessary.

Lenders including FirstRand had lobbied heavily for the compensation scheme to be watered down. The FCA did make some changes to the scheme following feedback from industry, but the South African group said that this was outweighed by other “problematic” amendments.

FirstRand’s higher payout estimate came as a result of the watchdog’s decision to broaden the scope of what it considered to be an “unfair” loan and changes to the amount of interest on unfair loans that a company would have to pay back to customers.

Back in October, the FCA had said it expected average payouts to total a lower sum of £700.

The South African bank said that it reserved its legal rights to challenge the scheme and that it believed that the FCA’s terms were “deeply flawed” and diverged “significantly and inappropriately” from a previous Supreme Court ruling.

The total cost for FirstRand would far exceed the £275m in profits that it has made on its motor finance activities in the UK over the past decade.

An FCA spokesman said: “Our scheme provides certainty and is the most cost efficient and orderly way to deal with liabilities that exist, no matter what. We welcome lenders, First Rand included, making financial provisions so they can deliver it.

“The market remains resilient – a record £41bn was lent on motor finance in 2025 and February’s new car sales were the highest in 22 years. With reforms now in place, we do not see further major redress events on the horizon.”

data.title: Britain’s stock market eclipsed by Taiwan on AI boom
data.description:
Britain’s stock market has been eclipsed by Taiwan as the island’s tech firms benefit from an AI boom.

The total value of all Taiwan-listed companies rose to $4.14tn (£3tn) on Wednesday, climbing above the UK market, which is valued at $4.09tn.

It follows an AI-fuelled surge in valuations of companies in the country. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSMC), the chip manufacturer that accounts for nearly 40pc of the value of Taiwan’s market, bounced back to record highs after a momentary dip driven by the Iran war.

The chip giant reported a 35pc increase in quarterly revenue this week. It said it expects sales to grow by almost a third in dollar terms this year. Demand for TSMC chips has been bolstered by the boom in AI spending, which the war in Iran has done little to dent.

Meanwhile, the country’s Taiex index has shrugged off the impact of Middle East disruption. Earlier this week, it erased all the losses it had accrued since the war started on Feb 28.

Taiwan’s market is the latest in a series to have overtaken the UK. Back in 2023, India’s market surpassed Britain’s, thanks to its rapid economic growth, and Canada’s market, which has a similar reliance on banks and commodities, has also jumped in front of the UK.

Twenty years ago the UK’s value was double Canada’s and triple India’s.

It comes amid wider concerns that London’s public market is losing ground to its rivals. Since 2021, there have been few major listings in the UK and a number of major businesses have instead moved their main listings abroad. Over the past decade, the number of companies listed in London has shrunk by a quarter.

Wise, the UK’s second-biggest tech company, announced it would move its listing to the US in June last year, while Flutter Entertainment, the gambling group, made the shift in 2024.

The UK’s FTSE 100 has risen by less than 4pc this month, as concerns about inflation and high interest rates have held back British businesses.

In its latest World Economic Outlook, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) cut its estimate for UK growth this year to 0.8pc, from the 1.3pc prediction made in January before the Iran war began.

The IMF said its downgrade was because of the war, which would lead to fewer interest rate cuts and higher energy prices. As a net importer of energy, the UK remained especially sensitive to a rise in energy prices, the fund said.

The downgrade was the largest of the world’s advanced economies, with the UK economy now expected to grow less than France, Canada and the US, but more than Japan and Italy. The UK is also expected to have the joint highest inflation in the G7 this year at 3.2pc.

However, some parts of London’s stock market – namely its energy and mining stocks – have benefited from rising oil and commodity prices. These industries now account for almost a fifth of the total UK market cap.

A survey of fund managers in April found that the UK was the second-most favoured market in Europe after Switzerland.

However, a net 16pc of global fund managers say they are underweight British stocks, compared with 15pc last month.

data.title: End of Saturday post moves closer as unions drop opposition
data.description:
Royal Mail will push ahead with plans to scrap second-class letter deliveries on Saturdays after striking a deal with its powerful union.

The postal service has reached an agreement with the Communication Workers Union (CWU), which represents more than 100,000 postal staff, to end a protracted stand-off over planned reforms to its delivery model.

Royal Mail has been trialling changes that include ending Saturday deliveries for second-class post, which it argued was essential to securing its financial sustainability.

While the CWU supported the changes in principle, the union had argued that the trials were not working and accused postal bosses of being in “denial”.

Earlier this year, Royal Mail issued an ultimatum in an attempt to break the deadlock, launching a formal dispute resolution process and threatening to press ahead with the changes regardless unless a deal was reached. In response, union bosses threatened to walk out.

However, the two sides have reached an agreement on a working pattern that will see deliveries carried out on alternate days.

Some 240 delivery offices will start trialling the new model, with full rollout expected across the country by the end of the year.

Royal Mail has also agreed to hand a 4.75pc pay rise and enhanced terms to staff who joined the company after December 2022. It followed complaints from the union that the postal service was struggling to recruit and retain new entrants because of unequal pay.

CWU bosses unanimously endorsed the deal, which will be put to a vote by members.

If confirmed, the deal would allow Royal Mail to press ahead with long-mooted changes to its universal service obligation (USO), which requires it to deliver anywhere in the UK six days a week. Bosses said these requirements had been unsustainable amid a sharp drop in letter-sending.

In addition to scrapping second-class letter deliveries, regulator Ofcom has agreed to relax Royal Mail’s quality-of-service targets. The postal service has been fined more than £36m over the last three years for repeatedly missing its targets.

Royal Mail is under intense political pressure over its deteriorating quality of service. The company is on track to deliver 219 million letters late this year, even as customers have been hit with repeated increases in stamp prices.

Daniel Křetínský, the Czech billionaire who bought Royal Mail for £3.6bn last year, was summoned before MPs last month, who called the postal service a “national institution in meltdown”.

Mr Křetínský defended the company’s performance and denied widespread claims that it was prioritising its more lucrative parcels business over letter deliveries, which are in decline.

Alistair Cochrane, the chief executive of Royal Mail, said: “This agreement with the CWU paves the way for universal service reform rollout and represents a significant investment in our people.

“Moving ahead with reform will make a real difference to Royal Mail’s quality of service, supporting the delivery of a reliable, efficient and financially sustainable postal service for our customers across the UK.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: British banks to be given access to AI ‘too dangerous to release’
data.description:
British banks will be given access to the most advanced AI hacking bot from Anthropic after it was deemed too dangerous to release to the public.

The Silicon Valley AI lab confirmed UK financial institutions would be added to its Project Glasswing programme, through which companies are given early access to Anthropic’s Claude Mythos artificial intelligence (AI) tool to stress-test their cyber defences.

Pip White, Anthropic’s UK chief, said the scheme would expand to British businesses “in the very near term, in the next week”.

She told Bloomberg Television: “As you would expect, the engagement that I have had from UK CEOs in the last week has been significant.”

A source familiar with the programme said they expected several UK businesses to sign up to the second phase of the project.

Banks and technology companies have been racing to shore up their cybersecurity systems after Anthropic revealed the capabilities of its Mythos bot. The AI software could autonomously find previously unknown bugs in software that could expose companies to devastating cyber attacks.

Anthropic said its AI tool had found flaws in every major technology operating system and internet browser faster than would be possible for a human hacker.

The $380bn (£281bn) company has delayed the launch of the tool to the public, resulting from its powerful cyber capabilities. It has so far released Mythos to a select group of tech giants, including Apple and Microsoft, as well as US banks such as JP Morgan, so that they can strengthen their security.

The Telegraph revealed last week that the Bank of England was preparing to warn City chiefs about the powerful new AI technology in upcoming security briefings.

The UK’s AI Security Institute, the Government’s testing lab, confirmed this week it had trialled the new technology, warning it was “a step up over previous frontier models”. The AI can link together cyber attacks that require multiple steps, similar to a human hacker, in a job which would typically take professionals “days of work”.

Ms White said Anthropic had delayed the release of Claude Mythos and put its “own limitations around this product because we know how powerful it can be”.

“If we have to slow down the release for the safe and responsible development of AI and that has a commercial impact, we will do that.”

The disclosure came as Anthropic confirmed it would be reinforcing its UK presence, opening a larger London office early next year with space for 800 people.

Anthropic has about 200 staff in London. The AI lab recently raised $30bn in new funding to expand and has been linked with an initial public offering as soon as this year.

The London expansion was revealed as Anthropic grapples with a rift with the White House. The US Department of Defense labelled Anthropic a “supply chain risk” after it refused to approve its technology for use in fully autonomous weapons.

Anthropic has sued the White House over the blacklisting, which would force US government departments to stop using its AI tools. The case is ongoing. UK government officials have attempted to woo Anthropic to expand its London presence amid the split with Donald Trump’s regime.

Ms White said: “London is already one of our most important research and commercial hubs outside the US, and our expansion in the knowledge quarter gives us the room to grow into.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Shoe brand’s shares soar as it reinvents itself as AI provider
data.description:
Struggling shoemaker Allbirds has sought to reinvent itself as an AI provider in an attempt to safeguard its future.

The once-fashionable brand, once valued at $4bn (£3bn) and worn by the likes of Leonardo DiCaprio and Barack Obama, said on Wednesday it would abandon consumer products in favour of supplying data centres.

This will include providing cloud computing capacity and focusing on “AI compute infrastructure”.

The announcement was enough to turn Allbirds into a “meme stock”, as shares soared by more than 300pc when markets opened.

The San Francisco-based brand was once considered a trailblazer for environmentally friendly fashion, using eco-friendly materials like merino wool and eucalyptus fibres in its apparel.

However, the company’s sales have tumbled in recent years, leading to the closure of its last full-priced US stores in February.

Founded in 2015, Allbirds rode a wave of endorsements from high-profile celebrities keen to bolster their eco credentials. In 2018, Oprah Winfrey put them on her “favourite things” list.

Other early adopters included actors Gwyneth Paltrow and Emma Watson, and Jacinda Ardern, the former New Zealand prime minister.

Allbirds was floated on Nasdaq in 2021, reaching a peak of $27 a share on the first day of trading. It has sold more than one million pairs of its original merino wool trainers. However, its shares have plunged by 99pc in recent years as sales fell away.

Last month, the company announced it would sell its intellectual property and other assets for $39m to American Exchange Group, which said it would continue to sell products under the Allbirds brand.

Allbirds will now continue under the name NewBird AI, the company said on Wednesday. The company also announced that it had secured a $50m investment towards the pivot.

A spokesman said: “The company will initially seek to acquire high-performance, low-latency AI compute hardware and provide access under long-term lease arrangements, meeting customer demand that spot markets and hyperscalers are unable to reliably service.”

The surprise announcement boosted the company’s stock price from $3 to over $10 per share.

The pivot is reminiscent of the Bitcoin boom, when several companies rebranded as cryptocurrency plays to stoke investor interest.

That includes New York-based Long Island Iced Tea, which renamed itself Long Blockchain Corp, and Kodak, which briefly announced a foray into blockchain technology.

data.title: Inside the forgotten utopia overlooking the Thames Estuary
data.description:
On the southernmost edge of Essex, a stone’s throw from the muddy banks of the Thames Estuary, there’s an incongruous sight: rows of blocky white Bauhaus-style homes, along with vast rectangular buildings that blink with hundreds of panes of glass. 
This is the town that Bata built.

For 72 years, the occupants of those modernist homes, now part of East Tilbury, made shoes for the world. At its height in the Sixties, 3,000 people were employed here by the Czech footwear giant Bata. In 1964 alone, more than 12 million pairs of shoes were made at East Tilbury, making it the biggest shoemaker in the country.

While almost half were exported, Bata also made footwear for the Scouts, for British children whose parents shopped at Mothercare, and, during the war, combat boots, plimsolls and socks for the British Army.

Among the wellies, Derriboots and tennis shoes were some curious creations. There were Wayfinders for Scouts, featuring the footprints of British animals in their rubber soles, plus a compass in the heel; Wayfinders Moonshot, issued in 1969, with a lunar landscape on the sole and the lunar landing module on the heel; and “Rain Beauties”, a transparent ladies’ overboot which tried to seduce women with its name.

But as innovative as the shoes Bata produced in Essex was the utopian model village – Bata-ville – created for its workers, which included modernist homes, an open-air swimming pool, an on-trend espresso bar, and even its own newspaper. Many of these relics, I recently discovered, can still be seen today.

Building Bata-ville

Czech cobbler Tomáš Bat’a, ramping up operations for his growing global shoe empire, headquartered in Zlín, snapped up 620 acres of Essex potato fields in 1932. However, just six months after the purchase, he died in a plane crash, leaving his half-brother Jan Bat’a to pick up the reins.

Britain was in the grip of the Great Depression, with around 25 per cent of the workforce unemployed as 1933 dawned – so Bata had no trouble finding British staff to join the Czechs it brought in. Desperate job hunters came on foot – and, later, by rail – from across the country.

The first of 13 factory buildings opened in the middle of 1933, and it was cutting-edge. Bata introduced conveyor belts to its production line after Tomáš had witnessed Ford cars being manufactured in America.

Away from the factory floor, a utopia for workers on the Thames Estuary was devised by František Lydie Gahura and Vladimír Karfík, a pair of Czech architects who considered themselves disciples of urban planning pioneer Le Corbusier. Their vision manifested itself in Bata-ville’s 222 flat-topped cubic homes, the very first painted in clotted cream with “wild sage” windows and doors.

For recreation, workers were given an outdoor pool, tennis courts and that unlikely-for-rural-Essex, à la mode espresso bar. Practical matters were taken care of with a clinic, a supermarket, a market garden and a dairy farm that produced Bata-branded bottles of milk. A shop sold the factory’s own shoes at a discount.

The Bata Village football team managed to strike a deal with West Ham United for an annual charity match and, in 1955, even emerged victorious, beating the Hammers 3-1. West Ham’s players would later train on Bata’s pitch before going on to win the FA Cup Final in 1964.

As the company grew, pitch-roofed houses, some with balconies, and fancy managers’ homes were built. Five-storey Stanford House began life in 1936 as a community venue where workers lunched in a 1,000-seater subsidised canteen on dishes such as mock turtle soup, minced beef and barley, and sago pudding with custard. Converted to a hotel in 1957, it welcomed Tom Jones in 1963, who crooned for party-goers in the ballroom.

But it wasn’t all fun and games in the hotel. Guests needed to heed a diktat: “IN NO CIRCUMSTANCES IS ANY PERSON PERMITTED TO VISIT THE BEDROOM OF A PERSON OF THE OPPOSITE SEX,” shouted the printed rules.

A matter of record

When love did flourish in Bata-ville, marriages were celebrated with a picture in the weekly Bata Record, which ran from 1934 until 1991, thoroughly tracking the village’s goings-on. Some locals feared it was all a bit Orwellian. Along with marriages, there’s a record of who went down with the flu, who was off work, bereavement notices, car accident injuries, and scores for darts and billiards matches.

The factory closed in 2005 due to rising labour costs and changing tastes in footwear, but the old village – now part of East Tilbury – doesn’t have the plaintiveness of a place that has lost its purpose. When I visited, schoolkids bounced around, retirees mowed lawns, and pushchairs gathered on pavements.

The five-storey rubber factory, a Grade II-listed building with a water tower, still bore the red Bata logo but now houses Lemon Fresh UK (turning out Spanish cleaning products) in what’s now called the Thames Industrial Park. The neat listed administration building displayed a fine collection of shoe lasts (moulds) in the foyer. A statue of Tomáš Bat’a looked over his former Essex empire.

Apartments and shops fill the former hotel, while the cinema is now the East Tilbury Village Hall. The Espresso Bar, now covered in tatty hoardings, was, sadly, unrecognisable.

“I used to go there to hang out and listen to the jukebox in the Sixties,” former Bata worker Mick Pinion, 76, told me as we toured the estate on a sunny day. England flags fluttered on poles in the light breeze coming off the Thames, a mile to the south.

Mick spent his childhood on Bata Avenue, where the first flat-topped houses were built; was a firefighter in his long-haired youth at Bata’s own fire station (which used a Skoda engine driven all the way from Zlín); and served as a maintenance technician for 40 years in Bata’s utopia.

A strict regime

We walked to the memorial commemorating the 83 Bata-ville residents who died in the Second World War, and Mick pointed out fencing marking the edge of the village, just beyond.

“At the top is barbed wire. Most fences were built to keep people out but here the barbed wire sloped inwards to keep you in,” Mick joked as we were joined by his former workmates John Purkiss, 73, and Graham Sutcliffe, 74.

An exaggeration, of course, but it seems that Bata managers were hard taskmasters. Mick and John quipped that Graham, with “only” 21 years’ service to Bata, was a “deserter”.

“The regime was very strict,” confirmed John, who worked as a toolmaker for 37 years. Workers had to be at their machines by 7.30am. Late arrival meant docked pay.

“Marching music was played for going in and more sombre music on the way out,” John said.

Mike Tarbard, 79, and chairman of the Bata Heritage Centre, whose father worked as a Bata electrician and foreman, recalled the story of an apprentice whose tasks were timed with a stopwatch. When he came of age, he left to join the British Army “because it was easier”.

“As most of the system was run on conveyors, if one person wasn’t there, it would slow [everything] down,” added Mick. “So, you didn’t only get flak from the supervisors but also from fellow workers as you’d lose them money.”

Too many slip-ups and you’d be out and your tied housing lost, the men recalled.

“Mr Bat’a was [treated] like a god,” John remembered. “Whenever he visited a factory, everything was painted clean.”

Ahead of its time

But for all the discipline, Bata was ahead of its time: part-time work for women fitted around school hours, rubber tyres were recycled from 1935, and food waste was collected in bins for the farm pigs… The estate was largely self-sufficient – the only thing imported was leather (but even then, Bata owned a tannery in Leicestershire).

Back at the excellent Bata Heritage Centre in East Tilbury Library, where the men volunteer, preparations were under way for an upcoming open day on April 25 (another will be held in October). This year also marks 150 years since Tomáš Bat’a’s birth, and commemorative events will be held at the Czech Centre in London from April 9 to June 12.

What was life like growing up in Bata-ville, I asked the gents.

“Other children were very jealous,” said Mick. “We had the swimming pool, the sports field… we had everything here.”

John added: “I remember my friend’s mum tying a snow sledge to her car and driving us around the estate. I also had a good mate of mine who was a Czech, and we’d make a beeline for his house as his mum was such a good cook.”

Big smiles erupted with the talk of sponge and strudel. “Oh… the Czech cakes,” chorused the men.

“And the apple strudel,” said Mick, wistfully. “Don’t forget the strudel!”

Claire travelled to East Tilbury with Greater Anglia and C2C. See Bata Heritage Centre for details of open days and walking tours.

data.title: The robot revolution will turbo-charge growth. But not in Britain
data.description:
“Zelda the welder”, as she has become known at her sheet metal factory in Kent, was an expensive robot. All in, when she was installed five years ago, the bill was £250,000.

But her owner Contracts Engineering, which has since installed a second one, has made that back multiple times over. The firm’s profits before tax have quadrupled.

The robotic welders have meant that the company has been able to beat international competitors. It has expanded its business so much that it has hired two additional human welders to work alongside the machines.

Today, though, Contracts Engineering is in the minority. Its approach is precisely what most factories in the UK are not doing.

When it comes to automation in manufacturing, a toxic cocktail of dismal business investment, a significant skills gap, sky-high energy costs and a fear of taking risks mean the UK is flailing far behind the rest of the world.

Performance is plummeting relative to other countries and factories are under threat if they cannot catch up.

In South Korea, there are 1,012 industrial robots for every 10,000 manufacturing workers. In the UK, the figure is roughly a tenth of this at 111, according to industry body Make UK.

That is roughly half the EU average of 208 and about a quarter of Germany’s figure of 415.

British manufacturing workers have 47pc less machinery, tools and technology than those in similar economies, according to analysis by the Institute for Public Policy Research (IPPR).

This disadvantage is hitting the UK’s global standing. Between 2005 and 2023, the UK plunged from 10th place in the United Nations Industrial Development Organization competitive industrial performance index, which measures a nation’s ability to produce and export manufactured goods, to 19th.

The UK’s share of global manufacturing has slumped from 3.1pc in 2000 to 1.9pc in 2022 while its share of exports has plummeted from 3.7pc to 1.5pc.

The opportunity cost is huge. Make UK estimates that if British manufacturers caught up with the technological advancements of their global competitors, it would add 5pc to the UK economy by 2035 – equivalent to an extra £150bn.

Automating is increasingly essential if UK manufacturers want to compete with overseas companies, says Catherine Barratt, the chief executive of BAMUK, which owns Contracts Engineering.

“The speed of adoption overseas is really high. The UK really needs to adopt automation at a higher pace and at a greater scale.”

Britain is missing out on several core elements of modern manufacturing that are now standard in leading industrial economies, says Faye Skelton, the head of policy at Make UK.

In many countries overseas, integrated smart factory systems such as “manufacturing execution systems”, real time production monitoring and digitally-connected tools that link machines with inventory data and schedules are the norm.

They allow businesses to make their factories far more efficient and respond rapidly to any kind of disruption. In the UK, these systems are largely lacking.

Collaborative robots (“cobots”) that work alongside people, automated guided vehicles and AI-enabled quality inspections and predictive maintenance systems are widespread in countries such as Germany and Japan. UK uptake is patchy.

Where businesses do invest, it is often incremental, buying a single robot rather than a whole factory transformation.

“We haven’t really progressed in terms of digitising our factories,” says Skelton.

“Only one in 10 manufacturers can be described as fully digitised. When you look at our competitors, such as Germany, we are significantly behind.”

The UK has lost out on particular sectors because it has not acted quickly enough or developed the digital technology needed to manufacture a product competitively, says Skelton. Wind turbines are a key example.

“Even though our universities were at the forefront of working out how to develop wind energy, we didn’t have the suitable manufacturers with the same level of tech and robotics in the UK to make it,” says Skelton. Today, turbines are manufactured primarily in China and Denmark.

As the gap between the UK and its competitors gets wider, the problem could become existential.

Pranesh Narayanan, a senior research fellow at IPPR, warns: “At some point, companies will start saying they might just have to shut down their factories because there are all these more advanced competitors in other countries.”

One key issue is the fact that UK business investment is the second lowest in the G7, only marginally ahead of Canada.

UK firms make investments worth just 11.1pc of GDP, according to IPPR. In Japan, by contrast, business investment is equivalent to 18.2pc of GDP.

Significant political instability over the last decade in the UK, which saw Britain leave the EU and multiple changes in governments and industrial policies, has deterred foreign investors.

But another issue is that local banks are reluctant to lend the money for companies to invest in new technologies where there aren’t years of evidence of reliable returns.

“The appetite is low for investment from banks. We hear it a lot from SMEs and it does sound like more of a UK problem,” says Skelton.

PP Control and Automation, a factory near Birmingham that builds robotics such as mechanical milking machines as well as the electrical panels used by other manufacturers to make other types of automation equipment, is booming.

Pinaki Banerjee, the chief executive, expects the company’s revenues will surge by 50pc this year as demand rises for automation equipment in the UK.

Yet even this is not enough to persuade him to continue focusing on Britain.

PP Control and Automation has no plans to invest in the UK in the years ahead. Instead, it is going to spend £30m buying up factories in Italy, India and the US over the next three years.

The crux of the issue is the lack of investment in the UK, says Banerjee. If more factories were planning to ramp up automation, PP would be looking at buying more assets in the UK.

“We’re backed by private equity, I have to also keep an eye on how I can maximise my valuation to my shareholders,” Banerjee says. “If I just expand in the UK, I don’t think I’ll get a bump on my valuation.”

Another big problem is high energy prices. The UK faces some of the steepest electricity costs in Europe, meaning higher costs for automation.

Firms say a national skills shortage is also crucial. Nearly half of manufacturers (46pc) pointed to a lack of skills as their biggest hurdle to automating in a Make UK survey.

The Government’s migration advisory committee suggested in a 2022 report that there could be a link between high levels of immigration during the UK’s EU membership and low investment in automation because it incentivised recruitment over labour-saving capital investments.

In the years since, immigration soared to unprecedented levels. But industry experts say that the UK’s minimum wage system stopped this post-pandemic flow from making labour cheaper and it did not fill the major skills shortage in the manufacturing sector, which currently has around 50,000 unfilled long-term vacancies.

Whatever the hold ups, the outlook is only getting worse.

Today, businesses are grappling with high taxes following Rachel Reeves’s increase in employers’ National Insurance payments and April’s business rates rise. They are facing higher employment costs following Labour’s Employment Rights Act 2025 and soaring energy costs as the war in Iran drives up oil prices.

All of this means the need for companies to make efficiency gains is becoming even more crucial. But Skelton says it is also making it harder for many to find the money to do it: “What our members are telling us is that they don’t have the cash to invest.”
