data.title: I visited Britain’s most battered train bridge. No wonder the railways are in chaos
data.description:
For people living near Watling Street in Hinckley, it’s not unusual to be woken in the night by a thunderous bang as another lorry comes to grief at the local railway bridge.

This small Leicestershire town is home to Britain’s most battered bridge. Surrounded by depots of enormous vehicles, it was struck at least 22 times by lorries and buses throughout 2024 and 2025.

Across the country, a railway bridge is hit by a road vehicle every five hours, with about 1,700 strikes on bridges recorded by Network Rail between April 2024 and March 2025.

The result was 120 days of delays to train journeys last year and a £23m bill for taxpayers, with each strike costing about £13,000.

Some 43 per cent of lorry drivers admit to not measuring their vehicle before setting off and 52 per cent do not take low bridges into account.

Outside Hinckley on the A5, heavy goods vehicles of all types, from concrete mixers to delivery vans, push their luck at speed, zooming eye-wateringly close to the underbelly of the bridge that bears the scars of past collisions.

Each time one crunches underneath the steelwork, mayhem ensues, with long traffic jams, train delays and debris littering the road, according to residents.

“It’s a nightmare,” one neighbour says. “It’s frightening, like a bang or an explosion. It happens all the time, at least once a week.”

The bridge’s luminous yellow-striped warning signs are visibly scarred by scrapes, while the supporting walls are missing large chunks of brick.

In the lay-by, splinters and vehicle parts lie scattered through the undergrowth and residents of a nearby caravan park report tables, chairs and even mattresses flying out of lorries.

A mother with two young daughters sighs as she recalls how the bangs shake the caravan almost weekly.

Chantelle Callton, 35, tells The Telegraph: “Oh my God, it shakes. It’s like a bomb’s gone off. Everybody speeds down here, so it’s very dangerous.”

The bridge is so notorious that she was warned about it more than six years ago before she moved there.

“If we go a week when it’s not been hit, we’ll say: ‘We’re due one soon,’” she adds.

Dianna Smith, 60, who runs the caravan site with her husband, says her son and his friend had to help a trapped driver out of a lorry in the night. “It’s a ticking time-bomb until something bad happens,” she adds.

“They get that much speed up and don’t realise they’re too high. One lorry’s top was pulled back like a tin opener. They have to stop the trains and it messes everything up.”

Betty Watton, 44, sitting outside with her two chihuahuas, lives closest to the bridge and says drivers often flee the scene.

She says: “One lorry tore the whole top off and just left it in the road, rolled up like a big piece of scrap. One time paint tins spilt and paint went everywhere, but it’s mainly parcels.”

At a nearby depot filled with HGVs and lorries, workers clad in orange hi-vis laugh knowingly at the mention of the bridge.

They refuse to speak, but one jokes to another: “How many times have you hit it?”

Vehicles over a certain height at the Greenway Environmental depot have to take lengthy detours to avoid the bridge, including driving on to the motorway, the assistant manager says.

Another worker suggests it might not be the most strategic location for a lorry depot.

“Earlier this year, one was full of papers, cardboard and packaging. When that got ripped apart, it peeled the lid back and paper and card flew everywhere, blocking the street off for quite a while,” he says.

“There are a lot of industrial sites and major warehouses here. The kind of places that have drivers that are coming here for the very first time. They follow their sat nav, but still...

“There are a lot of light-up signs the whole way saying: ‘High vehicle. Turn back.’ But it’s become quite an issue that lorry drivers use their phones while driving.”

Mountpark, which is funding the project and working with National Highways, has planned to lower the road under the bridge since 2023, but critics of the scheme fear it would be complex and lead to flooding.

Karen, the landlady of a nearby pub, says road works will only add to the traffic jams blighting her business.

With the construction of a new supermarket depot around the bridge, she fears that the problem will not be going away anytime soon.

data.title: Labour under fire for leaving builders in the dark about rewilding rules
data.description:
Projects such as railways, power stations and reservoirs risk delays because Labour has kept industry in the dark about new “rewilding” rules about to come into force.

Ministers have failed to provide details of how a new system requiring major developers to offset the damage they do to nature will work.

From next month, developers of large infrastructure projects will be required to make up for any nature they destroy by increasing the quality of natural habitats elsewhere, such as woodlands, by at least 10pc. The rules amount to a legal requirement for rewilding habitat for wildlife.

The rules are scheduled to come into force within weeks. However, builders and industry representatives say they have been left in the dark about how it will work.

Julia Baker, of Mott MacDonald, an engineering consultancy, said: “A mandatory policy for biodiversity net gain (BNG) will create a level playing field, which is welcomed. But the only thing we know is that it will be live in May, and it’s April.

“We have such long waiting times for development consent orders (DCOs) – you need to turn the wheel at least a year before you’re going to hit that corner.

“The frustration is that we really want to make it work, but with less than a month, the only thing that we can do is focus on compliance rather than all the benefits that BNG can generate.”

The Biodiversity Net Gain (BNG) Federation, a trade body representing companies delivering nature improvement under the BNG regime, has written to the Chancellor and the Housing Secretary demanding the immediate publication of guidance.

In the letter, the federation warned developers risk being stranded in a “regulatory vacuum” with less than a month before the deadline.

“NSIP developers tell us that their progress is being hindered,” wrote the trade body. “They cannot prepare without knowing what compliance will require.

“Without answers, the market remains on pause.”

They warned that Nationally Significant Infrastructure Projects (NSIPs) could face delays. These cover the biggest and most complex developments across the country, such as airport expansions, reservoirs and solar and wind farms.

Biodiversity rules were introduced in 2024 for smaller projects, but NSIPs have been subject to a separate timetable given their scale and complexity.

The warning comes at an uncomfortable moment for Rachel Reeves, with the speeding up of major infrastructure projects central to her growth agenda.

This included a vow last year to cut red tape so developers could “stop worrying about bats and newts” and unlock a construction boom.

Ms Baker said she was concerned that the rules were designed for smaller housing developments, rather than large projects.

“It’s 10pc, so if your baseline is one hectare or your baseline is 100 hectares, it really makes a difference,” she said.

“When you’re working on a 200km pipeline, you can spend a lot of time on just survey work, rather than investing that money in nature restoration.”

A government spokesman said: “We are committed to biodiversity net gain, as it plays a vital role in protecting nature while building the homes this country needs.

“We’ve heard clearly from developers, local authorities, and ecologists that the system needs to work better for some of the smallest developments, which is why we have announced plans for a new 0.2 hectare area exemption.

“Government will publish full responses to last year’s consultations shortly, setting out further detail, including how BNG will operate for Nationally Significant Infrastructure Projects.”

data.title: Can investors still bank on the big banks?
data.description:
Questor, The Telegraph’s investing column, takes a weekly view of the markets – what is moving them, what lies ahead and how all of this could affect your portfolios and financial goals.

If there was one sector which investment banks (“sell side”) and institutional fund managers (“buy side”) agreed was a smart investment this year, it was the banks.

While there were notable, contrarian exceptions, the tone of most commentary on the big lenders was positive.

It is proving a more volatile year than anticipated – not least thanks to the war in the Middle East – but even in this context, investors could be forgiven for being disappointed with the sector.

After a multi-year bull run, the FTSE 350 Banks index is down in the year to date. At the time of writing, it sits just 16th out of the 38 industrial groupings that comprise the FTSE 350.

In view of the old market saying – “a ‘bad’ stock in a ‘good’ sector will outperform a ‘good’ stock in a ‘bad’ sector” – it is worth reviewing the bull and bear case for the banks.

Good times

Analysts expect banks to generate almost a quarter of the FTSE 100’s forecast aggregate pre-tax profit for 2026 and pay out a fifth of its dividends. Lenders matter to the UK equity market as much as the supply of appropriately priced credit helps to grease the gears of the nation’s economic engine.

In this respect, it is easy to see why the banks’ shares had done so well.

First, the big lenders have earned their way back into investors’ affections. Their combined pre-tax profits came to £50.7bn in 2025, a record high, and analysts have pencilled in further increases for 2026 and 2027.

Second, the big banks have also paid their way back into investors’ affections with bumper cash returns in the form of both dividends and share buybacks.

The total return via the combined mechanisms in 2025 was £31bn, the second-highest sum ever from the big five (Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, NatWest and Standard Chartered) and equivalent to a cash yield of 7.5pc based on their current stock market valuations.

Finally, at the start of the year at least, the macroeconomic environment looked benign. 

Interest rates were declining slowly, structural hedges locked in healthy net interest margins while loan losses were muted, regulatory fines were limited and – for those with investment banking operations – financial markets were buoyant.

That combination underpinned analysts’ forecasts for higher profits and dividends in 2026 and 2027.

Hard times

This year’s drop in the FTSE 350 Banks index might be a blip, but there are concerns investors need to consider as they assess the risk-reward profile of the banks’ shares.

First, the stocks are no longer as cheap as they were, thanks to the fact that they have done so well.

Each of Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, NatWest and Standard Chartered began this decade trading at big discounts to tangible net asset value whereas, at their highs earlier this year, they all traded at a premium to book value.

In addition, only NatWest’s forecast dividend yield currently exceeds the 10-year gilt yield.

Second, HSBC has brought its buyback programme to a halt while it digests its $13.6bn (£10.1bn) purchase of the 37pc stake in Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Bank that it did not already own. 

NatWest has also declared a pause after its current programme following its £2.7bn swoop for Evelyn Partners.

That may mean total buybacks from the banks decline for the second year in a row in 2026 and the markets are proving sensitive to this shift in momentum, not least as they may prefer the safety of buybacks to the risks associated with acquisitions.

Finally, the macroeconomic environment looks less certain, mainly thanks to the war in the Middle East.

Interest rates may stay higher for longer to support net interest margins but any economic slowdown because of higher energy prices could lead to an increase in loan losses that offsets the extra interest income.

In addition, banking stocks are weak in the US and Europe – albeit again after a long, storming run.

Some of this may be because of fears over the lenders’ exposures to private credit and private equity – either through their loan books or their investment banking operations – where they have them.

The lenders continue to rebut such suggestions and assert that a couple of high-profile corporate failures in the US are part and parcel of the business of high-yield lending.

Some of it may just be wider worries about inflation, interest rates and above all growth, should the Middle East war last for longer than hoped and oil and gas prices stay elevated as a result, to the detriment of consumers’ cash flows and corporate profit margins.

A speedy resolution in the Strait of Hormuz could at least remove one worry and give the banks the chance to prove that they can be reliable providers of profits and dividends, two decades on from the first rumblings of the Global Financial Crisis.

data.title: Which banks make it easiest to access money after a death
data.description:
When someone dies, their bank account is typically frozen as soon as the provider has been notified. This is to make sure the money doesn’t get into the wrong person’s hands, but it can also leave relatives unable to pay for immediate expenses.

While many banks allow money to be released for payments such as funeral costs and inheritance tax, how quickly you can access the remaining funds depends on the provider.

Some banks will release relatively large sums without requiring a grant of probate, while others set far lower limits. The government-backed National Savings & Investments (NS&I), for example, will only release up to £5,000, whereas many high street banks allow up to 10 times that.

These thresholds apply to the total amount held with each provider and can make a big difference to how easily relatives can cover costs.

Here, Telegraph Money compares the thresholds set by major banks, and explains how the process works.

How much money will banks release before probate is required?

The table below outlines how much money some of the major banks will release before requiring a grant of probate.

The most flexible providers are those with £50,000 thresholds, such as Barclays, Nationwide, NatWest and Santander.

At the other end of the scale, NS&I has one of the lowest limits at £5,000, while digital banks such as Monzo and Starling also set relatively low thresholds.

What is the process when someone dies?

When someone dies, their bank should be notified as soon as possible – usually by a next-of-kin, the executor named in the will (if there is one), or another personal representative.

You can contact each bank separately, either online, over the phone or in-branch, or sign up to the free Death Notification Service which notifies participating financial institutions in one go.

Banks will typically ask for a copy of the death certificate and proof of identity for the executor or administrator, such as a passport or driving licence. The account will then be frozen, meaning most payments will be stopped.

Whether you can release any funds after this, and how quickly the account can be closed, will depend on the provider’s rules and how much money the account holds.

David McGuire, of law firm Weightmans, said: “Many people assume that there is a fixed rule about how much money can be released after someone dies without the need to apply for probate.

“Typically, and historically, this was considered to be a concession for estates worth less than £5,000. However, in reality, there is no universal threshold. Each bank takes a pragmatic approach and sets its own limit through internal guidelines.”

This can make it particularly complicated if there are multiple accounts and providers involved. According to Mr McGuire, it is not uncommon for one bank to release funds relatively quickly, while another takes a more cautious approach and requires probate before doing so. The process is also different for joint and sole accounts.

Mr McGuire said: “Joint accounts are usually more straightforward, with funds passing automatically to the surviving account holder, meaning those funds can often be accessed without delay. Sole accounts, however, are where the discrepancies can arise.”

If funds can be released without a grant of probate, the personal representative or executor will need to sign an indemnity form and provide ID.

What happens if grant of probate is required?

Probate is the legal process of dealing with someone’s estate after they die, including confirming who has the authority to manage their finances and distribute their assets according to their will (or the law if there is no will).

This often involves applying for a grant of probate, which many banks require before they agree to release larger sums of money. To do this, the executor of the will must apply to the Probate Registry. If there is no will, a close relative can apply for Letters of Administration.

The application process typically involves completing forms, valuing the estate, and calculating and paying inheritance tax. While it is possible to do this yourself, some people choose to use a solicitor to manage the process.

Applying for probate can take several weeks or even months, during which time access to funds may be limited.

Olivia Meekin, of law firm Bishop & Sewell, said: “This can cause financial difficulties for the personal representatives, who often have to continue paying the running costs of the deceased’s property (such as insurance, service charges and utility bills).”

After probate has been received, the bank will release the remaining balance to the personal representatives named on the grant of probate. The account can then be closed.

Do banks offer additional bereavement support?

Additional support will vary depending on the bank, but many offer dedicated bereavement teams and services to help executors or next of kin. You will usually find a bereavement section on each bank’s website to outline the steps you must take, and what the process will involve.

A Barclays spokesman said: “Where it helps reduce administrative burden, customers can also submit documents online, including completing and signing the settlement form digitally.

“We can also support with practical matters, such as releasing funds to help cover funeral costs and other priority bills, before accounts are fully settled, where appropriate.”

Meanwhile, HSBC says it offers end-to-end case support, guiding executors or personal representatives through each stage of the process and helping them understand what’s needed as part of estate management.

A spokesman for the bank added: “This includes explaining next steps, helping you gather any required information, and supporting you through the practical requirements involved in managing the deceased’s affairs with HSBC.”

Some banks can also help set up an executor’s account to help administer the deceased’s estate. But, like probate thresholds, the level of support can vary significantly between providers.

data.title: Reeves hands Rolls-Royce £600m to build mini-nukes in Britain
data.description:
Rolls-Royce has been handed a £600m loan from Rachel Reeves to help accelerate the development of Britain’s first mini-nuclear power plant.

The Chancellor’s National Wealth Fund confirmed details of the borrowing facility on Monday, which will be used to build three small modular reactors (SMRs) at Wylfa on the Welsh island of Anglesey.

The manufacturing giant was selected as the Government’s preferred partner for the project last year.

However, design work will now begin after Rolls-Royce struck an agreement with Great British Energy – Nuclear (GBEN), the quango set up to manage the mini-nuclear programme.

GBEN said the contract would unlock the next stage of the project, which aims to help commercialise nascent SMR technology.

It will provide Rolls-Royce with funds to complete detailed design work, including regulatory applications and planning approvals.

The £600m loan is in addition to £2.6bn that Ms Reeves allocated to the scheme during last year’s spending review.

Construction of the power plant itself is not expected to begin until the early 2030s, with the facility set to come online in the mid-2030s. But GBEN is understood to be carrying out ground preparation works now.

The scheme is eventually expected to create 3,000 local jobs in Wylfa, Anglesey, as well as another 5,000 supply chain jobs across the country.

Ms Reeves said: “This investment, along with vital financing from the National Wealth Fund, will strengthen our energy security, create skilled jobs and help to build a new generation of homegrown nuclear technology that will power our economy for decades to come.”

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, added: “At a time of global instability, this is a major milestone for Britain’s energy security.

“We are backing a British company to deliver our first small modular reactors – creating a generation of good jobs, driving growth and providing clean, home-grown power for decades to come.”

GBEN said the contract with Rolls-Royce stipulates that the company must hit various “milestones”, although it did not immediately clarify what those were.

The three mini reactors are expected to have a combined capacity of about 1.4 gigawatts, enough to power three million homes for more than 60 years.

SMRs will be constructed from “modules” built in factories and then transported to sites for assembly.

The idea is meant to ensure the plants can be produced efficiently at scale and more quickly than larger nuclear projects.

In the longer run, it is hoped their lower price tags will also make them more attractive to private investors, who have long viewed bigger plants as too expensive and risky to back without government support.

Major technology companies, including Google, Amazon and Facebook-owner Meta, are investing in potential SMR technologies in the hope they could eventually be used to supply power-hungry data centres needed to develop AI.

Rolls-Royce is also building six SMRs for the Czech government.

Simon Bowen, the chairman of GBEN, said: “This is an immense moment for the UK nuclear programme, our organisation and the industry as a whole.

“Today represents a true recognition of the efforts made across GBEN, Rolls-Royce SMR and Government to get to this point, and I’m hugely proud of the team for reaching this critical milestone.”

Chris Cholerton, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce SMR, added: “This contract unlocks the delivery of our first three units and brings certainty to the UK SMR programme.”

data.title: Why housebuilders have given up on London
data.description:
London is not building. In the first quarter of this year, 23 of the capital’s 33 boroughs started work on precisely zero new homes. How has a city, notorious for rising rents and housing shortages, ended up in this position?

London’s social housing waiting lists are at their highest level in more than a decade, with 336,000 households registered – a quarter of the national total.

The average first-time buyer in the capital faces a price tag of £500,000, while private rents have risen two thirds in the past 15 years to an average of £2,271 a month. This is 65pc higher than the national average, according to the Office for National Statistics.

And yet the industry that could boost the supply of housing and ease the pressure has all but downed tools.

Several forces are fuelling the problem: a labyrinthine and laughably complex planning system, a building safety regulator that spent years delaying applications, tax changes that have decimated investor returns, and the end of a government scheme to help first-time buyers get on the ladder.

Private sector housing starts in the capital have plummeted 84pc since 2015, according to data compiled by consultant Molior London. Just 5,547 private homes were started in London last year, against a target of 88,000, and compared to almost 34,000 in 2015.

London is supposed to deliver 440,000 of the Government’s proposed 1.5 million new homes this Parliament. At current rates of delivery, that target looks fanciful.

A Kafkaesque planning nightmare

London’s planning environment is the most challenging in the country. Of the 10 local authorities in England with the worst approval rates for residential planning applications, seven are in London.

The capital operates under not one, but three layers of planning oversight: national policy, the London Plan, and 33 separate local authorities, each with its own requirements, its own politics and its own interpretation of what a new development should deliver.

Paul Rickard, chief executive of Pocket Living, an affordable housing developer active in the capital, describes the level of red tape faced by schemes his company submitted recently.

By the time the application hit the planning portal, it ran to 89 documents, cost £1m and required 27 consultants. A recent project in Hackney generated 9,000 pages of planning documents.

Rickard argues that planning requirements should be tailored much more closely to the size and nature of projects to avoid unnecessary costs and bureaucracy.

“If you’re doing a six-storey building in central London, why do you need an aviation study?” he asks. “There is no point in spending the money. Planes are not going to crash into this building.”

For a small developer, this is not a straightforward hurdle to clear. A failed application can run to seven figures, and a quarter of smaller schemes must go through two or three planning applications.

“How many small developers can afford a million quid and then go, ‘well, that’s okay, I’ll just try again?’” Rickard asks.

Throw in vocal Nimby movements across the capital, and the result is a politically difficult climate to build new homes in. Council majorities in London are narrow – sometimes built on a 19pc share of the vote – and opposition to development from existing residents tends to be louder than support from those who would benefit from new homes being built.

A new regulator and a fresh wave of delays

Into an already Kafkaesque environment, the Building Safety Regulator (BSR) arrived in 2022. While its purpose – to prevent another Grenfell – was impossible to dispute, its execution has left the sector reeling with costly delays.

At its worst, the BSR was rejecting more than two thirds of pre-construction applications and taking an average of 36 weeks to process those it approved, against a target of 12 weeks.

Of 193 new-build high-rise applications submitted since the regulator’s founding, just 15 had successfully cleared the process by the time of publication of a Telegraph Money report in October 2025.

For London, where the density of homes means that the BSR’s scope is particularly broad, the effect on new starts has been severe.

However, since The Telegraph’s damning report on the BSR, there has been progress. New leadership has overhauled internal processes and Rickard now regularly hears of approvals coming through in 12 to 14 weeks. Developers have begun adjusting their financial assumptions accordingly.

“A year ago, they might have said, ‘I need to budget 12 months for [one step of the process] because I just do not know,’” he says. “They no longer need to do that.”

But three years of backlog cannot be undone in months, and Kate Butler, policy director at the British Property Federation, points out that despite improved approval times, there remains an underlying uncertainty among investors and developers.

A regulator that has demonstrated it can take a year or more to respond – even if it now does so faster – leaves lingering doubt for investment decisions.

An unattractive environment for investors

Tax changes and regulation have made matters worse. Previously, an investor buying a block of 10 flats was able to pay stamp duty based on the average price of each unit, rather than the total value of the entire building, thanks to multiple dwellings relief.

But in 2024, the Treasury scrapped this, and now a developer buying a £5m block of flats is taxed as if they are buying one £5m mansion, pushing them above the highest stamp duty threshold. This single change wrecked the viability of many London housing projects, wiping billions from the sector overnight.

The Renters’ Rights Act and the abolition of Section 21 “no-fault” evictions have also introduced fresh uncertainty about underwriting rental income, as tenants gain the right to challenge increases through a tribunal system that is already under strain.

“How you’re able to underwrite or predict any rental growth that you’re going to get is another [challenge],” says Butler.

Underlying all of this is a land market that refuses to correct.

In theory, when the cost of building goes up, landowners should drop their asking prices to keep development viable. In practice, those who don’t need to sell simply don’t – they hold on for better prices, or pass the land down to the next generation.

Pocket Living ran the numbers on what land would need to cost today for it to be able to build on the same financial terms as in 2020. Accounting for doubled finance costs, rising build prices and new regulatory levies, the answer was a 78pc fall in land values.

In reality, Rickard says that prices have dropped by around 40-50pc. While this is a meaningful correction, it is still far short of what developers need to make the sums work.

Inadequate support for first-time buyers

For the first time in 60 years, there is no government first-time-buyer support scheme. This is yet another obstacle for developers because it is much more difficult for aspiring buyers to purchase homes.

Mortgage rates have also jumped in recent years, making it even harder to borrow for a home purchase, and London rents have risen to levels at which saving a deposit is, for many working Londoners, simply out of reach.

Mortgage income multiples, set in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis, were not designed for a housing market in which rents in many parts of London exceed equivalent mortgage payments.

“We have people going from paying £1,500 a month in rent, all in, to owning their home for £1,400 a month,” says Rickard. “It is not necessarily that people cannot afford to buy. It is that they cannot access buying. That is a subtle but very important difference.”

Without buyers, developers cannot sell, and without sales, there is little incentive to build. Developers overwhelmingly argue that if first-time-buyer support were restored, construction activity would swiftly increase.

Pro-development Government fuels optimism

It is worth noting that the current Government did not create this crisis. Much of what is broken was done before it took office, and both Butler and Rickard are broadly supportive of Labour’s ambitions: major planning reforms, pro-development messaging and an ambitious new homes target.

“They are the most pro-development and open government for well over a decade,” says Rickard.

But a housing crisis of such a scale requires more than goodwill and posturing; Rickard would like to see a more aggressive removal of the countless layers of cost and bureaucracy.

“We need to be bolder, and we need to do it more quickly,” he adds.

When asked if Labour is on track to hit its new homes target, Butler is diplomatic.

“I think it’s optimistic and ambitious,” she says. “It’s hard to tell how close they’ll get or if they will hit it in this Parliament. But I do think there are things that they can do, and if they do them with urgency, they will see a big increase in development.”

data.title: Town centres face losing their car parks after operator’s collapse
data.description:
Scores of car parks across Britain face closure following the sudden collapse of National Car Parks (NCP), in a development that threatens to deal a hammer blow to town centres.

Administrators for NCP, the country’s biggest operator, are threatening to abandon sites if landlords refuse to accept drastically lower rents.

Up to 20 car parks have already been shut and councils are now braced for dozens more closures across NCP’s remaining 318 sites.

Closures threaten not only to leave landlords out of pocket but also deal a blow to councils, which risk missing out on millions of pounds in business rates. An unoccupied building owned by a company in administration is typically exempt from business rates.

The closure of NCP car parks would also be a blow for town centres. Many are the main sites for people visiting shopping centres, meaning visitor numbers could suffer.

Insolvency experts at PwC and CBRE are currently scrambling to safeguard NCP’s future after it collapsed into administration this month.

This first involved stripping out loss-making sites across the company’s estate before then demanding lower rents from its landlords. Administrators are now seeking a buyer for the business.

However, the process has already been frustrated by tensions with landlords who are furious at administrators’ threats to close sites if terms cannot be agreed. In some cases, landlords are being asked to reduce rents by more than 50pc.

As well as seeking significantly cheaper rates, administrators have also demanded a four-week rent waiver to help put the company on a stable footing.

Landlords have been given just days to agree to new terms on sites occupied by NCP, increasing the likelihood of widespread closures.

One landlord said they were given 24 hours’ notice of the new offer and told to accept or face immediate closure.

“We view these last-minute bullying tactics as the lowest form of negotiation and are not prepared to do business with people who behave in this manner,” they said.

City lawyers are already in contact with landlords in the event of potential legal action, with some considering whether to take sites back from NCP.

It is understood that the sites already closed have effectively been abandoned, including those in Birmingham, Leicester, London and Luton.

One landlord said they were not even told the site had been closed.

It has sparked alarm among city councils over fears that empty car parks will become a magnet for anti-social behaviour.

Some local officials are already emailing landlords to ask what security measures will be put in place to keep sites safe.

The importance of car parks to local economies has also prompted speculation that some councils may have to foot the bill for reopening sites to support businesses. Such a move would increase pressure on already cash-strapped local authorities.

The developments will trigger scrutiny of NCP’s former Japanese owner, Park24. The private equity firm blamed a combination of Covid, high rents and elevated energy costs for NCP’s downfall, while PwC said the company came unstuck as “significant rent payments” fell due.

However, others have pointed to spiralling borrowings across the business, which rose to £350m by the end of 2025. Some City sources said these debts were largely to blame for the company’s collapse.

NCP charged high prices and had limited overheads, suggesting it should have been financially stable without debt. In central London, NCP charges motorists as much as £60 a day for parking.

Following their appointment, PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Administrators said last night they were still working hard to sell some or all of the business, which was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before merging with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

A spokesman for the administrators said: “A small number of commercially unviable sites have closed this week and while no other closures are currently planned, the administrators are engaging with key stakeholders, including landlords of some sites, to reach agreements that will allow for their continued operation.”

data.title: Tesco cannot guarantee shoppers will be shielded from food shortages
data.description:
The boss of Tesco has said he cannot guarantee that shoppers will not experience food shortages this summer.

Ken Murphy, the chief executive of Britain’s biggest supermarket, said it “would be wrong” for him to guarantee that customers will not see supply issues as a result of the situation in the Middle East.

“Can I provide guarantees? Of course, not,” he told The Telegraph.

“Given how quickly the situation globally changes and the volatility of the actors involved in this conflict, I think [it] would be wrong for me to give you any guarantee.

“I’m giving you a commitment that Tesco will do its very best to minimise the impact on customers, and I think that we’ve demonstrated that commitment over the last five years.”

Mr Murphy was speaking after it emerged the Government has drawn up contingency plans for potential food shortages if the war in Iran continued.

The Times revealed that officials have conducted an analysis for a “reasonable worst-case scenario” where carbon dioxide would be in short supply, which could result in shortages of chicken and pork.

The analysis is based on the Strait of Hormuz not being reopened by summer and no permanent peace deal.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed planning for possible food shortages was under way on Thursday and said Sir Keir Starmer was “leading from the front”.

Mr Kyle told Times Radio: “Of course, we don’t comment on leaks. But this is now out there. But I want to reassure people that we have been acting since the very start of this conflict.”

He added: “We are looking at a variety of scenarios... When people read what is a leak, and it is difficult for me because these leaks are very unhelpful, but when people read it, they need to be reassured that we are doing this planning and we are doing this kind of scenario planning.

“I can tell you because I’m in these meetings that the Prime Minister has been there since the start of this war and he is going through and driving deep dives into resilience in lots of areas in our economy. You have a Government that is acting with creativity and boldness.”

Mr Kyle insisted food shortages were currently “not a concern” and said he would be “upfront” with the public if that were to change.

Mr Murphy said the Government was doing “the right thing” in drawing up contingency plans, adding: “I think the Government is planning and scenario-managing worst-case scenarios, and then saying, how would we cope in that scenario?”

He said Tesco was not currently experiencing any supply issues, with none of its farmers or growers having flagged problems.

Mr Murphy said: “We are constantly working on various scenarios ourselves internally, as you would expect us to do. None of our suppliers have flagged any issues to us at this point. I think that’s an important point.”

He said he welcomed the Government decision last month to reopen a Teesside carbon dioxide production facility, saying this was “really good news”.

The National Pig Association, which represents farmers and processors of the animals, said it was “concerned” about the impact of the war in the Middle East.

“If the production and therefore availability of CO2 is restricted, we would expect this to impact supply,” a spokesman said, calling for the meat industry to be prioritised for supply of the gas.

data.title: Banks deepen links with private credit amid crisis fears
data.description:
Banks are deepening their links to the murky world of private credit amid growing alarm about risks posed by the sector.

High street lenders struck deals with the so-called “shadow banks” at a record pace last year, The Telegraph can reveal.

Banks lined up 27 so-called “significant risk transfer” (SRTs), where private credit funds are paid to bear the loss from certain bank loans if borrowers default.

It marked the highest number of SRTs in the last five years, up from 24 deals in 2024.

The deepening links come amid growing alarm about the private credit industry, often referred to as “shadow banking”.

In the private credit industry, loans come not from banks but from sources such as hedge funds and financiers. It has grown rapidly since the financial crisis because it is more loosely regulated than bank lending.

Andrew Bailey, the governor of the Bank of England, has compared the risks in the sector to the subprime mortgage market before the 2008 financial crash. Problems in that relatively obscure corner of finance triggered a global crisis.

Unlike mainstream lenders, shadow banks are largely unregulated. The sector has grown rapidly since the financial crisis and is now a $3tn (£2.2tn) industry.

However, a series of high-profile failures of shadow bank-backed businesses and a suspected fraud in the UK have raised concerns about loose lending standards and poor due diligence. That has triggered scrutiny of links between banks and private credit funds.

Prof Raghavendra Rau, of Cambridge Judge Business School, said: “How much exposure the regulated banks have to lightly regulated non-bank financial institutions is opaque.

“And worse, because private credit lacks public market discipline, the losses can be magnified, meaning unexpected shocks could cascade through the financial system rapidly before regulators even see them coming.”

SRT deals are the main link between high street banks and private credit. They have become wildly popular in Europe, with Barclays and Santander amongst the biggest players.

In a typical SRT deal, a bank pays a private credit fund to assume the risk of default for a group of mortgages or company loans.

The arrangement allows banks to hold less cash in reserve to guard against loans going bad, freeing it up for fee-earning activity.

In the UK, each SRT must be approved by the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA), the banking watchdog. According to a Freedom of Information request, banks sent 27 deals for approval by the regulator in 2025. In 2024, 24 deals were sent for approval and 19 were notified to the PRA in 2023.

Some regulators have grown increasingly concerned about the practice, amid broader scrutiny of shadow banks.

In a report released by the International Monetary Fund last year, the global watchdog warned that the rapid growth of SRTs could “increase vulnerabilities” to financial stability, “including higher leverage in the financial system”.

It also warned that risks were exacerbated by the “private and opaque nature” of the transactions themselves.

In some cases, private credit funds borrow money from other banks to fund SRT deals. Some fear that this is setting up the risk of a house-of-cards-style collapse that could ricochet across the banking industry if a significant number of loans start to go bad.

Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JP Morgan, has said that there could be more “cockroaches” in the private credit sector after the collapse of Tricolor, an auto lender, and First Brands Group, a car-parts supplier. Both were heavy users of private credit.

In the UK, the sector has been shaken by the collapse of Market Financial Solutions (MFS), a private credit fund in the property industry that has been mired in allegations of fraud. Lawyers representing Paresh Raja, the company’s founder, admitted to mistakes but denied any intention to defraud.

The Prudential Regulation Authority was contacted for comment.

data.title: BP hails ‘exceptional’ oil trading during Iran war
data.description:
BP has hailed an “exceptional” performance at the start of the year after cashing in on surging oil prices during the Iran war.

The energy giant said it expected trading revenues to jump during the first three months of 2026 owing to “heightened volatility in crude oil, natural gas and refined products prices”.

This represents a significant rebound for BP after a weak performance in the last quarter of 2025, demonstrating how the energy shock unleashed by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is benefitting the world’s biggest oil and gas giants.

At least a fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies rely on the crucial trade artery.

The International Energy Agency (IEA) said global oil demand dropped sharply following the outbreak of the Iran war amid soaring prices and a shortage of supplies from the Middle East.

Figures from the IEA’s monthly oil report showed that global demand was down 3.4pc in March. It is expected to decline a further 1.1pc in April to 100.4 million barrels a day, which would be the lowest level in three years.

The IEA warned that “demand destruction will spread as scarcity and higher prices persist”.

BP highlighted the impact of surging oil and gas prices in its latest update, noting that the average price of a barrel of Brent crude hit $81 in the first quarter, up from $63 in the final three months of the year.

The oil price peaked at nearly $120 last month, although it is now hovering around $100 after the US and Iran struck a two-week ceasefire last week.

Blow to financial results

BP said the rapid changes in market conditions were “expected to impact financial results”. It added that oil production and operations would be “slightly lower compared to the fourth quarter 2025” after its operations in the Middle East were affected by the conflict.

Around a fifth of BP’s overall oil production is generated in the Middle East, although it does not operate any refineries in the region.

Other oil companies have been caught up in the conflict after their refineries were damaged by drone attacks.

The conflict represents the first challenge for Meg O’Neill, who took over as BP’s chief executive earlier this month.

She became BP’s third boss in just two and a half years following the departure of Bernard Looney after failing to disclose romantic affairs with colleagues, while her predecessor Murray Auchincloss was ousted late last year.

Ms O’Neill told staff that BP will be simplified into two main business units, moving the company back to the way it was structured before its ill-fated attempt to invest in green energy in 2020.

She said that one division of the group will focus on oil and gas production, while another will cover BP’s refining, distribution and retail activities.

In line with its current strategy, Ms O’Neill said BP would continue to redirect billions of pounds from green energy projects towards oil and gas in an attempt to increase profitability.

Shell said last week that it expected its oil trading business to report a boost in earnings following the rapid increase in oil prices.

data.title: Supermarkets ‘taking the mickey’ on olive oil prices, says Filippo Berio
data.description:
The boss of Filippo Berio has accused supermarkets of “taking the mickey” out of customers with their sky-high olive oil prices.

Walter Zanre told Sky News that wholesale olive oil prices had fallen in recent months but said supermarkets were refusing to follow suit.

“We brought prices down twice last year and it’s not all been passed on to the consumer, which is a huge frustration,” he said.

It follows a surge in the price of olive oil since 2022, after adverse weather in olive-growing countries led to poor harvests.

Spain, which produces almost half of the world’s olive oil, was hit by droughts and heatwaves in 2023 and 2024. The weather caused global olive oil production to fall by almost one million tonnes.

It caused higher prices for many well-known olive oils, including Filippo Berio, which uses a blend of oils from across Europe despite being an Italian brand.

The average price of a 500ml bottle of Filippo Berio has risen from £3.75 in 2022 to £7.50 now, according to the tracker Trolley.co.uk.

Tesco’s own-brand olive oil stands at £5.50 for 500ml. This is up from £5.20 this time last year, Trolley.co.uk data shows. The same-size bottle of Nicolas Alziari olive oil, at Waitrose, sells for £22.50.

The boss of Filippo Berio argued that prices should have come down rapidly for customers after stronger production more recently meant there was better availability of oil.

According to the International Council of Olive Growers, the wholesale Italian olive oil price was down 30pc in March compared to last year’s figures.

‘Expand their margins’

Mr Zanre, who has worked for the olive oil brand for more than 25 years, said producers had reduced the price at which they sell to supermarkets, but that this was not being seen on the shelves.

He said: “We can’t dictate retail prices. For me, it’s immensely frustrating that supermarkets have taken the opportunity to expand their margins.”

Mr Zanre accused supermarkets of profiteering from strong demand for olive oil, suggesting grocery bosses had probably been surprised at how resilient olive oil sales had been in recent years, even though it was more expensive, and so had decided not to drop prices.

His claims prompted a backlash from retail leaders on Tuesday, who argued that they passed on reductions where they could.

Andrew Opie, the director of food and sustainability at the British Retail Consortium, said: “Retailers work hard to pass on cost savings to customers wherever possible and, as confirmed by the Competition and Markets Authority, operate on very tight margins, reflecting a market driven by savvy customers.

“Olive oil, like many everyday products, is something shoppers can compare across brands and retailers to take advantage of promotions or switch to alternatives that suit their budget.”

The attack against supermarkets comes amid fears that prices for olive oil could soon start rising again.

The war in Iran, and consequent fuel crisis from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, is expected to hit olive growers because they need diesel for their tractors as well as to fuel water pumps which irrigate the olive groves. It means production costs will probably rise this season.

It will add to pain for shoppers, who have been told to brace for higher prices across supermarket shelves. The Food & Drink Federation warned earlier this month that food inflation could hit 10pc by the end of the year, up from early forecasts of 3.2pc.

It said the war was having a “direct and immediate impact” on costs for UK food and drink manufacturers.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Axel Springer wins approval for Telegraph takeover
data.description:
The incoming owner of The Telegraph has revealed ambitions to make it “a truly international brand” after his £575m takeover was waved through.

Mathias Döpfner, the chief executive and controlling shareholder of the German news publisher Axel Springer, assured staff that The Telegraph will retain its “British identity” and that after almost three years of ownership limbo, it “should now take off”.

He welcomed the decision by Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, to approve the sale without further delays.

Mr Döpfner said: “We are pleased to have received UK government approval to proceed with this acquisition. After a long period of uncertainty, we are able to confirm that we will invest significantly in The Telegraph’s editorial excellence and international growth.”

It came after Ms Nandy told MPs that she had given permission for the Abu Dhabi-backed investor RedBird IMI to sell security in The Telegraph to Axel Springer. The company also owns the specialist titles Politico and Business Insider, as well as scores of print and online publications across Europe.

Mr Döpfner said: “The Telegraph will join a global network committed to independent reporting while retaining its distinct editorial voice and British identity.”

In a memo he told staff he would personally take charge of The Telegraph on Axel Springer’s executive board, calling it “a great privilege – and an even greater responsibility”.

Mr Döpfner added: “What connects us with The Telegraph is not only a shared commitment to freedom and centrist conservative values, but also a distinctive blend of tradition and progress. Both of us have consistently approached technological change and the opportunities of the media industry with a forward-looking mindset.”

The security was set up in 2023 to be immediately converted to full ownership. It means that Axel Springer could be in effective control of The Telegraph within days.

Mr Döpfner, 63, and a journalist by background, sought to reassure staff and readers about Axel Springer’s values and commitment to editorial independence.

He said: “Protecting the newsroom by a clear separation between commercial and editorial decisions – this principle is deeply embedded in our corporate DNA. Decisions about what is published rest with the editors-in-chief. That’s non-negotiable.

“Does that mean that anything goes? No. Our values define the framework. As a journalist and a committed democrat, I hold strong personal views, which I express openly in my writing and in discussion.”

Mr Döpfner added: “However, they do not constitute instructions for our journalists – and everyone who works with us understands this. 

“I value debate in the spirit of pluralism and freedom of expression – especially with our editors-in-chief, all of whom are independent and strong-minded. And sometimes we agree to disagree.”

The decision by Ms Nandy to allow the transfer of security in The Telegraph came alongside a preliminary decision not to trigger a regulatory investigation that would have imposed further months of delay.

She said she was “not minded to” involve the Competition and Markets Authority or Ofcom. The regulators used to scrutinise media takeovers where there are competition or public interest concerns.

Axel Springer is Europe’s largest news publisher, with decades of heritage in independent journalism via German major titles including the tabloid Bild and broadsheet Die Welt. 

Its English-language publications Politico and Business Insider have small market shares in the UK, and lawyers for the company have argued that they do not threaten competition or media plurality.

Ms Nandy did not announce a final decision and reserved the right to change her mind if new information comes to light.

But she said: “I am pleased to be able to take these positive steps, which give greater certainty to The Telegraph and its staff.”

Axel Springer also requires regulatory approval in Ireland and Austria, which may take some weeks. However, the British decision is the critical one and lays the ground for Axel Springer to take effective charge of The Telegraph very soon.

The news was greeted with relief in The Telegraph newsroom. It will all but end a period of damaging uncertainty which began on June 7, 2023, when the Barclay family’s unpaid debts caught up with them.

Lloyds Banking Group seized control of The Telegraph because of £1.2bn in overdue lending to its proprietors, who also owned the parcel courier Yodel and online retailer Very.

The bank planned an auction of The Telegraph, which was derailed by a deal between the Barclay family and the rulers of the United Arab Emirates. The Gulf state paid off its debts in exchange for security over The Telegraph via RedBird IMI, which it majority funded.

Its plan to take full ownership was blocked following a campaign against the takeover by The Telegraph and a cross-party outcry, which led to a ban on foreign state ownership of newspapers.

Without it, The Telegraph would have covered the Iran conflict under the ownership of the UAE. In recent weeks, its authorities have clamped down on free speech, including jailing British visitors for social media posts.

Baroness Stowell of Beeston, a Conservative peer who played a key role in legislating against UAE ownership, said: “Finally, The Telegraph’s ownership has been settled and it is just such great news.

“Here’s to a great future and continued press freedom.”

The decision to block RedBird IMI set off a diplomatic row and a torturous form of corporate captivity, as a series of onward sales were agreed and aborted.

They included one deal with a previously unknown New York publisher and one with the US private equity firm RedBird, which was the UAE’s junior partner. It planned to allow the Gulf state to retain a 15pc stake after Labour relaxed the ban on foreign-state shareholdings. The Telegraph also campaigned against those plans.

When they collapsed in October, Lord Rothermere, the owner of The Daily Mail, agreed to buy The Telegraph in a deal that was set to face lengthy regulatory scrutiny. 

Then, finally, last month, Axel Springer swooped in with a knockout bid of £575m, which appears set to glide through the political and regulatory gauntlet.

The price represents a profit on the £500m paid by RedBird IMI and exceeds most analysts’ estimates of The Telegraph’s value in a rapidly shifting news landscape.

Mr Döpfner arrives on Fleet Street as an established international force. He was appointed chief executive of Axel Springer in 2002 and previously bid for The Telegraph when it was last sold, in 2004.

The company, founded in post-war Berlin, has successfully expanded under his leadership and has said it aims to become the free world’s leading news publisher. Its corporate “constitution” promotes freedom, free speech, free markets, the rule of law and democracy.

Mr Döpfner told The Telegraph staff that he suspected that the company’s eponymous founder “deep down, he would have liked to have been English”.

“In a sense, he now is – four decades after his death,” he added.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
